The Library of Forgotten Things was a cavern. Endless shelves stretched in every direction, disappearing into darkness above and below. Books of every size and material lined the shelves — some bound in leather, some in skin, some that seemed to be made of light or shadow or memory. The silence was absolute. Not even her heartbeat made a sound.

Xiao Qing walked forward. The shelves rearranged themselves as she moved, creating paths that appeared and vanished behind her. She was being guided. Or herded.

After what felt like hours, she reached a clearing in the center of the cavern. A single table stood there, carved from black stone. On it sat a book. The book was open, its pages blank.

"I've been expecting you."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. Xiao Qing spun, but there was no one there.

"You're the green snake," the voice continued. "Sister to the white. Daughter of the mountain. Wielder of the jade pendant. You killed a celestial guard four days ago. You watched an elder burn to ash. You carry his bone in your sleeve."

Xiao Qing's hand went to her sleeve. "Show yourself."

"Patience. I've been patient for six hundred years. You can wait a few more moments."

A figure emerged from between two shelves. It was a woman, or had been once. Her body was translucent, flickering like a candle flame in wind. Her eyes were white, blind. Her hair hung in ropes down her back, tangled with dried flowers and small bones.

"I am the Librarian," she said. "Welcome to my home."

"You're a ghost."

"I'm a remnant. A memory given form. The library preserves what the world forgets, and in doing so, it preserves me." The Librarian drifted closer, her feet not touching the ground. "You seek information about your sister. About the original registry."

"Yes."

"The information exists. But nothing in this library is free." The Librarian gestured to the open book on the table. "The library will ask you a question. You must answer truthfully. If you lie, you will be trapped here, becoming another forgotten thing. If you refuse to answer, same result. If you answer truthfully..."

"What happens?"

"Then I will give you what you seek." The Librarian's blind eyes fixed on Xiao Qing's face. "Are you ready?"

Xiao Qing looked at the book. The blank pages seemed to pulse, hungry. She thought of Bai Suyin, sealed under Leifeng Pagoda. She thought of Elder Xu, burning in the suppression runes. She thought of five centuries of waiting, of training, of rage held in check like a coiled spring.

"I'm ready."

The Librarian nodded. The book's pages flipped, faster and faster, until they stopped on a single page. Words appeared, written in fire:

**What do you fear most?**

Xiao Qing's breath caught. She'd expected a question about her sister, about her past, about the celestial court. Not this.

"The library always asks the question you most need to answer," the Librarian said softly. "Truthfully, now."

What did she fear most?

Failure. Losing her sister forever. Being too weak, too slow, too late. The same fears that had driven her for five centuries. But those were surface fears, the ones she'd acknowledged. The library demanded deeper.

Xiao Qing closed her eyes. And for the first time in five hundred years, she told the truth.

"I fear that I am not enough. That even after all this time, all this cultivation, all this rage—I am still the little sister. Still the green snake who follows. Still the one who is always second. I fear that when the moment comes, I will freeze. I fear that I am not worthy of my sister's love, or her sacrifice, or her memory. I fear that I am nothing but a shadow, and that when the light comes, I will disappear."

The words hung in the air, raw and bleeding.

The book absorbed them. The fire words faded, replaced by a single line:

**Truth accepted.**

The Librarian smiled. It was a sad smile. "You are more than you know, green snake. But that is not what you came to learn."

She reached into the shelf beside her and pulled out a scroll. It was yellowed with age, sealed with wax that bore the mark of the ancient celestial court — the one that had ruled before the current administration, before the purges, before the rewriting of history.

"This is the original registry of demon cultivation," the Librarian said. "It records every being who achieved transcendence in the thousand years before the current court took power. Your sister is in here. So is Fahai. So are beings that have been erased from every other record in existence."

Xiao Qing reached for it. The Librarian pulled it back.

"One more thing. There is someone here who wishes to speak with you. Someone who has been waiting for a very long time."

"I don't have time—"

"You will make time." The Librarian's voice hardened. "Because this person knows something that is not in the registry. Something that no one else in the three realms knows. Something about Fahai's true purpose."

Xiao Qing's blood went cold. "Fahai's purpose was to destroy my sister."

"That's what he wanted you to believe." The Librarian turned and gestured for Xiao Qing to follow. "Come. She's been waiting for six hundred years. She can wait a few more minutes."

She. Xiao Qing's heart pounded. Who else could be trapped here, waiting for her?

The Librarian led her through a maze of shelves, deeper into the cavern. The air grew colder. The books on these shelves were different—older, darker, bound in materials Xiao Qing didn't want to identify.

Finally, they stopped before a cage.

It wasn't a physical cage. It was made of light and sound and memory, woven into a prison that held a single figure. A woman, sitting cross-legged on the floor, her face hidden by a curtain of white hair.

"Who is this?" Xiao Qing asked.

The woman looked up.

And Xiao Qing's world shattered.

The face that stared back at her was her sister's face. But older. Wiser. More worn. The eyes were the same deep brown, the same gentle curve of the lips. But there was a scar running from temple to jaw, and the left eye was clouded, blind.

"Little Qing," the woman said, and her voice was Bai Suyin's voice, but wrong, like a bell that had cracked. "You've grown."

Xiao Qing's legs gave out. She fell to her knees.

"Sister?"

"I'm not your sister." The woman's smile was bittersweet. "I'm her shadow. The part of her that was cut away when Fahai sealed her. He didn't just trap her body under the pagoda. He split her soul. Her light went into the tower. Her darkness came here."

"You've been here for six hundred years."

"Waiting for you." The shadow leaned forward, pressing her face against the bars of light. "Because I know the truth, little Qing. The truth about why Fahai really sealed your sister. It wasn't about the law, or demon cultivation, or the celestial court."

"Then what was it about?"

The shadow's smile widened, and it was terrible.

"He was afraid of her. Of what she was becoming. Of what she was meant to be." The shadow's voice dropped to a whisper. "Your sister wasn't just a demon, little Qing. She was a key. And Fahai didn't seal her to protect the world from her."

She reached through the bars, her translucent fingers brushing Xiao Qing's cheek.

"He sealed her to protect himself from what she could unlock."

Xiao

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Price of Memory

The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking through the mountain pass, following the faint thread of residual energy that Elder Xu had described in his final moments. The thread twisted and turned, doubled back on itself, sometimes vanished entirely for hours at a time. It was a trail designed to frustrate, to exhaust, to drive seekers mad.

She did not grow frustrated. She did not grow tired. She had spent five centuries learning to wait.

On the third night, the thread led her to a cliff face that appeared solid. She pressed her palm against the stone and felt it—a heartbeat, slow and ancient, pulsing beneath the surface. The mountain was alive. The mountain was the door.

"Open," she said.

The mountain did not respond.

She pressed harder, pushing her qi into the stone. The heartbeat quickened, then stopped. The cliff remained solid.

Xiao Qing stepped back and considered her options. Elder Xu had mentioned a sacrifice, but he hadn't specified what kind. Blood? Qi? Memory? She knelt and placed both hands on the ground, closing her eyes.

"I am Xiao Qing," she said. "Daughter of the Green Dragon of the Eastern Sea. Sister of Bai Suyin. I have come for the original registry."

Silence.

Then, a voice like grinding stone: "What will you give?"

Xiao Qing's eyes opened. "What do you want?"

"Memory. The oldest one. The one that hurts."

She felt her chest tighten. The oldest memory. The one that hurt. She knew exactly which one it was—the day her sister had been sealed, the day Xiao Qing had stood at the base of Leifeng Pagoda, screaming until her throat bled, while Fahai's golden light consumed everything she loved.

"No," she said. "That memory is all I have left of her."

"Then you will not enter."

The heartbeat resumed, slow and dismissive. The mountain was done with her.

Xiao Qing's hands curled into fists. She could feel the jade pendant against her chest, still warm from Elder Xu's sacrifice. She could feel the piece of his bone in her sleeve, carrying the weight of his final words. She had come too far. She had lost too much.

"Fine," she said through gritted teeth. "Take it."

The mountain shuddered. A crack appeared in the cliff face, glowing with pale blue light. The crack widened, forming an archway that led into absolute darkness.

"Step inside," the voice said. "And remember."

---

She stepped through.

The darkness swallowed her completely. No light. No sound. No sensation of ground beneath her feet. For a long moment, she existed only as awareness, floating in nothing.

Then the memory hit.

She was young again. Barely a century old, still learning to shift between human and serpent form. Her sister stood beside her, tall and radiant, her white robes flowing like moonlight.

"Don't be afraid, Xiao Qing," Bai Suyin said, her voice gentle. "The mortal realm is beautiful."

They were standing on a bridge overlooking West Lake. It was spring. Cherry blossoms drifted across the water, and the air smelled of rain and earth. Xiao Qing remembered being afraid—the mortals seemed so fragile, so strange, their lives burning bright and brief like candles.

But her sister's hand in hers made everything safe.

Then the scene shifted.

The temple. The golden light. Fahai's voice, chanting words that made Xiao Qing's bones vibrate with pain. Her sister on her knees, chains wrapped around her wrists, the pagoda looming above them both.

"No!" Xiao Qing screamed, but she was small, too small, too weak. She threw herself at Fahai, and he flicked her aside like an insect. She hit the ground hard, tasted blood.

Bai Suyin looked at her. Even in defeat, even in chains, her sister's eyes were calm. "Live," she whispered. "Grow strong. Remember me."

The pagoda fell.

Xiao Qing screamed until her voice broke. She clawed at the ground until her fingers bled. She tried to shift, to become the green serpent, to attack, to die, to do anything—

But she was too weak. Too small. Too young.

The memory burned through her like poison, every detail razor-sharp. The sound of stone grinding against stone. The taste of copper in her mouth. The weight of five hundred years of failure pressing down on her chest.

She couldn't breathe.

"Enough," she gasped. "Please. Enough."

The memory faded.

She was standing in a vast chamber, shelves of jade and crystal stretching endlessly in every direction. Each shelf held scrolls, tablets, books bound in materials she didn't recognize. The air was cold and still, thick with the weight of accumulated knowledge.

The Library of Forgotten Things.

A figure stood before her—tall, androgynous, made entirely of pale blue light. Its face had no features, but she felt its gaze on her like a physical weight.

"The memory is taken," it said. "You have seven hours in the library. Find what you seek, or leave empty."

"Seven hours?" Xiao Qing's voice was hoarse. "I gave you my most precious memory for seven hours?"

"You gave me your most painful memory for entry," the figure corrected. "Time is separate. Each additional hour costs more."

Xiao Qing wanted to argue, but she didn't have time. She turned to the shelves, her eyes scanning the endless rows. "Where is the original registry? The one from before the current celestial administration?"

"Level three," the figure said. "Section seven. Row twelve. The registry is bound in white dragon leather and sealed with blood jade."

She ran.

The library shifted around her as she moved, shelves rearranging themselves, corridors appearing and disappearing. She learned the pattern quickly—the library was testing her, seeing if she was worthy of its secrets. She dodged a collapsing shelf, leaped over a chasm that opened at her feet, and rolled under a falling crystal pillar.

Level three. Section seven. Row twelve.

The registry was exactly where the figure had said it would be. A massive tome, bound in scales that shimmered with residual dragon qi. The blood jade seal pulsed with a slow, rhythmic light, like a heartbeat.

She reached for it.

The seal flared, burning her fingers. She pulled back, hissing. The seal was warded—not against entry, but against theft. She would have to read it here, in the library, under the watchful gaze of whatever guardian protected this place.

She opened the tome.

The pages were filled with names, arranged by generation and cultivation level. Celestial officials. Immortal masters. Demonic cultivators. Mortals who had achieved enlightenment. Each entry listed their lineage, their achievements, their contributions to the three realms.

And each entry had a notation—a single sentence, written in a script so old that most scholars had forgotten it, that recorded the truth of their death. Not the official version. The real version.

She found her sister's name.

*Bai Suyin. White Snake of Mount Emei. Achieved immortality at age 247. Known for: healing the sick, teaching the ignorant, loving a mortal.*

*Official cause of death: Sealed under Leifeng Pagoda for crimes against celestial law.*

*Actual cause of death: Executed by order of the Jade Emperor. Reason: Knowledge of the celestial court's true origins. All records of her existence are to be expunged.*

Xiao Qing's hands trembled.

Executed. Not sealed. Executed.

She had spent five hundred years believing her sister was alive, trapped, waiting. She had cultivated for five centuries to become strong enough to free her. She had killed. She had sacrificed. She had burned pieces of herself away.

And her sister had been dead the entire time.

The page blurred. She blinked, and felt tears sliding down her cheeks. She hadn't cried in four hundred years. She had thought the tears had dried up, that she had become something beyond such mortal weakness.

She was wrong.

"Who knew?" she whispered to the page. "Who else knew?"

She kept reading.

The entries around her sister's name told a story. Officials who had been reassigned. Immortals who had been demoted. A single name—Elder Xu—marked as "retired," with a note that he had been the one to suggest the "sealed" narrative to the celestial court.

Elder Xu. The man who had burned to ash in front of her. The man who had taught her to shape water. The man who had known the truth and carried it for five centuries.

He had tried to tell her. In his final moments, he had tried to warn her.

*Remember who you are,* he had said. *Not the little sister. Not the green snake.*

He had been trying to tell her that her sister was dead. That her mission was impossible. That everything she had done, everything she had become, was built on a lie.

She wanted to scream. She wanted to tear the library apart stone by stone. She wanted to find the Jade Emperor and wrap her serpent form around his throat and squeeze until his golden blood ran through her fingers.

Instead, she kept reading.

The entry below her sister's caught her eye.

*Xiao Qing. Green Snake of Mount Emei. Achieved immortality at age 98. Known for: loyalty, temper, cultivation of water techniques.*

*Official status: Alive. Wanted for questioning regarding the destruction of celestial property.*

*Actual status: Subject of a prophecy. The Green Snake will unmake the three realms.*

The words hit her like a physical blow.

*The Green Snake will unmake the three realms.*

She read it again. And again. The characters didn't change. The prophecy remained, stark and absolute, written in that ancient script that spoke only truth.

She was not supposed to save her sister.

She was not supposed to free Bai Suyin.

She was supposed to destroy everything.

"Is this why you let me in?" she said, her voice hollow. "Not for my memory. For this."

The figure of blue light appeared beside her. "The library does not manipulate. It only reveals."

"You knew I would find this."

"I knew you would find *something*." The figure tilted its head. "The prophecy is incomplete. There are other fragments, scattered across the three realms. The library holds one more, if you wish to purchase it."

Xiao Qing's jaw tightened. "What's the price?"

"Your cultivation."

She stared at the figure. "My cultivation? Five hundred years of work?"

"Your cultivation," the figure repeated. "Every technique. Every breakthrough. Every drop of qi you have accumulated. In exchange, you will receive the second fragment of the prophecy."

She looked down at her hands. Hands that had learned to shape water. Hands that had learned to heal. Hands that had learned to kill. Five centuries of discipline, of meditation, of pushing her body and spirit to their absolute limits.

All of it. Gone.

And for what? A prophecy that might tell her more about how she was supposed to destroy the world?

But if she didn't know the full prophecy, she couldn't change it. She couldn't fight it. She would be a puppet, dancing on strings she couldn't see.

"Take it," she said.

The figure raised a hand. Light gathered around Xiao Qing, cold and absolute. She felt her qi begin to drain, flowing out of her like water through a broken dam. Her cultivation fell away—golden core, then silver, then bronze, then nothing. She felt her body weaken, her senses dull, her immortality flicker like a candle in the wind.

She dropped to her knees.

Age hit her like a wave. She felt every one of her six hundred

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Weight of Forgotten Things

The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, then west, then north again as the pocket realm shifted around her like a living thing. The mountains blurred. The rivers ran backward. She passed the same twisted pine tree seven times before she understood—the library was testing her. Seeing if she was worthy. Or desperate enough.

She was both.

On the third night, she stopped running. She sat beneath that pine tree, pressed her back against its gnarled bark, and closed her eyes. The jade pendant pulsed against her chest, warm and steady. She'd been chasing the library like prey. But you didn't catch a pocket realm by chasing it.

You caught it by letting it come to you.

"I'm not leaving," she said aloud. "I'll sit here until I rot. Until the mountain swallows me. Until the celestial court finds me and drags me back to their prisons."

She opened her eyes.

The pine tree was gone. The mountain was gone. She sat in the middle of an endless hall, shelves stretching up into darkness so deep it seemed to swallow light itself. Dust motes hung in the air like frozen snow. And everywhere—everywhere—were books. Scrolls. Bundles of bamboo slips tied with silk cord. Stone tablets covered in characters so old they predated the celestial script.

The Library of Forgotten Things.

Xiao Qing stood slowly. Her breath misted in the cold air. The hall was silent, but not empty. She felt eyes on her. Many eyes.

"Who enters?" The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. Old. Dry. Like paper crumbling. "Who dares disturb what should not be remembered?"

"I am Xiao Qing," she said. "Green Snake of Mount Emei. Sister to Bai Suyin."

A long pause. The dust motes stopped moving.

"Ah." The voice was different now. Softer. Almost sad. "The little sister. We wondered when you would come."

A figure emerged from between two shelves. It was human-shaped, but wrong—too tall, too thin, its skin the color of aged parchment. Its eyes were inkwells, black and depthless. It wore robes made of bound pages, and when it moved, the words shifted on its sleeves, rearranging themselves.

"I am the Keeper," it said. "I guard what the heavens wish to burn."

Xiao Qing's hand went to her sleeve, where Elder Xu's bone rested. "I need to see the original registry. The one that predates the current administration. The one that—"

"Contains the truth of your sister's trial." The Keeper's inkwell eyes glimmered. "Yes. We know what you seek. Everyone who comes here seeks the same thing. They want to rewrite history. To prove that the White Snake was wronged. That the celestial court is corrupt."

"Is she wronged?"

The Keeper smiled. It was not a pleasant expression. Pages rustled. "The truth is not the same as justice. And the truth is not the same as proof. Are you prepared for what you might find?"

"I've been prepared for five hundred years."

"Five hundred years of preparation means nothing against five thousand years of celestial lies." The Keeper turned and gestured for her to follow. "Come. The registry is in the deep stacks. But be warned—the deeper you go, the more the library remembers you. And the more it remembers, the harder it is to leave."

Xiao Qing followed.

The deep stacks were not like the main hall. Here, the shelves were made of bone. The books were chained to their shelves. And the air—the air was thick with something that felt like memory, pressing against her skin, trying to seep into her pores.

She passed a book that whispered her name. Another that showed her face, young and green and terrified, the day her sister was sealed. She kept walking.

"You said the library remembers," she said. "What does it remember about me?"

The Keeper did not slow. "It remembers that you were always the loyal one. The fierce one. The one who burned brighter than anyone expected." It glanced back at her. "It also remembers that you are not the first of your kind to seek this place."

Xiao Qing stopped. "What?"

The Keeper stopped too. It turned fully, and for the first time, its face showed something like emotion. Worry.

"Five hundred years ago, another serpent came here. A white serpent. She asked the same question. Sought the same registry."

"That's impossible." Xiao Qing's voice came out raw. "My sister was sealed before I fled the temple. She never—"

"She was not sealed when she came here." The Keeper's inkwell eyes reflected the dim light. "She came before her trial. Before Fahai caught her. She knew what the celestial court was planning. She came here to find proof that would save her."

Xiao Qing's hands were shaking. "Did she find it?"

The Keeper was silent for a long moment. Then it reached into its robes and pulled out a scroll. It was old. Yellowed. Tied with a red cord so faded it was almost brown.

"She found this," the Keeper said. "And then she left. She told me to keep it safe. To give it to you when you came."

"To me? She knew I would come?"

"She said you would. She said you were the only one she trusted." The Keeper held out the scroll. "She also said to warn you. The truth in this scroll will change everything. It will burn the world you know to ash. And when the ash settles, you will be alone."

Xiao Qing took the scroll. Her fingers trembled as she untied the cord. She unrolled it carefully, afraid it would crumble to dust.

The characters were written in her sister's hand. She would recognize that brushstroke anywhere—the slight tremor in the downward lines, the way she pressed harder at the end of each stroke. Her sister always wrote with too much passion.

But the words—

*To my dearest Xiao Qing,*

*If you are reading this, I am gone. Not just sealed. Gone. The celestial court did not imprison me because I committed a crime. They imprisoned me because I discovered the truth about the Dragon Throne. The current Jade Emperor is not the original. He is a usurper. Twelve thousand years ago, he murdered the true emperor and took his place. The original registry—the one that predates the current administration—contains proof of this.*

*The registry is not a list of names. It is a history. A confession. Every emperor who has sat on the Dragon Throne has known the truth. And every emperor has helped hide it.*

*Fahai was not my enemy. He was a tool. The celestial court sent him to silence me before I could speak. They used him as they use everyone.*

*Do not trust the heavens, Xiao Qing. Do not trust the courts. Trust only what you can hold in your hands. Trust only what you have seen with your own eyes.*

*And if you can—forgive me. For leaving you. For not telling you the truth sooner. For making you wait five hundred years to know why I really fell.*

*I love you, little sister. I always have.*

*Find the registry. Find the truth. And then—burn it all down.*

Xiao Qing's vision blurred. The scroll shook in her hands. Five hundred years. Five hundred years of thinking her sister had been punished for loving a mortal. Five hundred years of rage at Fahai, at the celestial court, at the world.

And the whole time, her sister had been trying to expose a usurper.

She looked up at the Keeper. "Where is the registry?"

"It is not here."

"Then where?"

The Keeper's face twisted. For a moment, it looked almost afraid. "The registry was removed from the library three hundred years ago. It was taken by someone who claimed authority from the Dragon Throne itself."

"Who?"

"A woman. She wore celestial armor and carried a token that burned with imperial fire. She said she was relocating the registry for 'safekeeping.'" The Keeper's voice dropped. "I did not believe her. But I could not stop her. The token was real. The authority was real."

"What did she look like?"

"Tall. Dark hair. A scar above her left eyebrow. And her eyes—" The Keeper paused. "Her eyes were the same color as yours."

Xiao Qing went cold. The same color as hers. Green.

Green snake eyes.

"There's another," she whispered. "Another serpent."

The Keeper nodded slowly. "We thought you were the last. But if there is another—if one of your kind has joined the celestial court—then everything changes."

Xiao Qing looked down at the scroll again. Her sister's words burned in her mind. *Burn it all down.*

She rolled the scroll carefully and tucked it into her sleeve next to Elder Xu's bone. Two pieces of the dead. Two pieces of the truth.

"I need to find her," she said. "This other serpent. She has the registry."

"The registry is not just a book," the Keeper said. "It is a weapon. In the wrong hands, it could destroy the three realms. In the right hands—" It paused. "In the right hands, it could destroy only the guilty."

"Then I need to find it before she uses it."

"Or before she destroys it."

Xiao Qing turned to leave. The library's shadows stretched around her, grasping at her feet, trying to hold her back.

"Green Snake," the Keeper called. "Your sister said you would burn it all down. But she also said you would find a way to rebuild. Do not forget that."

Xiao Qing did not turn around. "I'll remember."

She walked out of the deep stacks, out of the bone shelves, out of the hall of memories. The library released her reluctantly, spitting her out onto a mountainside she did not recognize.

The sun was rising. The sky was pink and gold. Beautiful. Terrible.

She stood alone on the mountainside, holding her sister's last words, Elder Xu's bone, and a truth so large it threatened to swallow her whole.

There was another green snake. Working for the celestial court. And that snake had the one thing that could prove the Jade Emperor was a murderer.

Xiao Qing closed her eyes. She thought about burning. She thought about ash. She thought about the face of the woman who'd watched her escape the prison cell—the one who'd smiled and whispered *she's ready.*

That woman had known. She had known Xiao Qing would come to the library. She had known about the registry.

She had been waiting.

Xiao Qing opened her eyes. She looked at the rising sun and smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"Let's see who burns brighter," she said. "Me, or the throne."

She stepped off the mountain and vanished into the mist.

---

Deep beneath the celestial palace, in a chamber that had no doors, a green-scaled woman sat before a massive stone tablet. Characters glowed on its surface—names, dates, crimes, truths. The original registry.

She traced her finger over one name.

*Bai Suyin. Serpent of Mount Emei. Witness to the Usurpation.*

The woman smiled. She had the same face as Xiao Qing. The same eyes. The same sharp cheekbones.

But her smile was older. Colder.

"Hello, little sister," she whispered. "I've been waiting for you."

The tablet pulsed once, then went dark.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Price of Memory

The Library of Forgotten Things did not appear to those who sought it desperately.

Xiao Qing learned this over seven days of walking through mountains that shifted beneath her feet, crossing rivers that flowed backward, and climbing stairs that led only to more stairs. The pocket realm resisted her, pushing her away with gentle but firm insistence, like a current that would not let her swim against it.

On the eighth day, she stopped trying.

She found a cave high in the Kunlun foothills, where the air thinned to almost nothing and the snow fell in curtains of white silence. She sat in the mouth of the cave, cross-legged, and let the cold seep into her bones. She stopped reaching. She stopped searching. She simply sat, her breath misting in the frozen air, and waited.

Three more days passed.

On the third night, when hunger had become a distant memory and her body had begun to forget its own boundaries, she heard a voice. Not from outside. From inside.

*Why do you seek what was never lost?*

Xiao Qing opened her eyes. The cave was gone. She sat in a vast chamber filled with shelves that stretched upward into darkness, each shelf crammed with scrolls and books and things that might have been books once but had since become something else entirely. The air smelled of old paper and older dust, and beneath that, something metallic. Blood memory.

"Because it was lost to me," she said. "I need the original registry. The one that existed before the celestial court rewrote history."

A figure emerged from between two shelves. Old. Female. Wrapped in robes that seemed to be woven from shadows themselves. Her face was a map of wrinkles, and her eyes held the patient stillness of mountains.

"You are the green snake," the woman said. Not a question.

"I am."

"I have been expecting you. Though I did not expect you to arrive so... intact." The woman smiled, revealing teeth that were too sharp, too many. "Most who find this place have given up pieces of themselves along the way. A memory. A decade of life. A small death."

Xiao Qing stood. Her legs protested, but she forced them steady. "I have given enough."

"Have you?" The woman—the Librarian, Xiao Qing realized—circled her slowly, examining her like a curious child might examine a strange insect. "You still have your cultivation. Your memories. Your sister's jade pendant. Your rage." She stopped in front of Xiao Qing, close enough that Xiao Qing could smell the dust and metal on her breath. "You have not yet paid the price."

"What price?"

"The price of truth." The Librarian gestured at the endless shelves. "Everything in this library was deposited by someone who wanted to forget. Every scroll, every book, every fragment of recorded existence—it was given freely, in exchange for peace. The truth is heavy, little snake. That is why people bring it here. To lighten their load."

Xiao Qing's hand went to the jade pendant at her throat. "I'm not here to forget. I'm here to remember."

"Yes, yes." The Librarian waved impatiently. "They all say that. But the registry you seek was never meant to be found. It was sealed by the celestial court itself, locked with nine layers of prohibition, and hidden in the deepest vault of this library. To open that vault, you must give something of equal value."

"What do you want?"

The Librarian's smile widened. It was not a pleasant sight. "Your sister's final words to you."

Xiao Qing went cold. Not the cold of the mountain snow, but something deeper. Something that had been frozen inside her for five hundred years.

"I don't—"

"You do." The Librarian's voice dropped, becoming almost gentle. "You remember every syllable. Every breath. The way her voice cracked when she told you to run. The way she said your name, not Xiao Qing, but Qing'er, the name only she ever used. You remember, and you have carried that memory like a stone in your chest for five centuries."

"You want me to give it up?"

"I want you to deposit it here. In exchange for the registry." The Librarian spread her hands. "A fair trade. A memory for a truth. You will still know that she spoke to you. You will still know that she loved you. But the words themselves—the exact shape of them, the sound of her voice, the weight of that last moment—those will belong to this library."

Xiao Qing's hands were shaking. She pressed them against her thighs to still them. "That's all I have left of her."

"No." The Librarian's voice was firm. "That's all you have left of her *ending*. She lives in your bones, little snake. In the way you move, the way you fight, the way you love. Her ending is not her legacy. But if you cannot see that, perhaps you do not deserve the registry after all."

The insult was deliberate. Xiao Qing felt it land, felt the familiar burn of being dismissed, being told she was less than worthy. Five hundred years ago, she might have lashed out. Five hundred years ago, she might have attacked, demanded, raged.

But she was not that green snake anymore.

"I accept," she said.

The Librarian's eyebrows rose. "Just like that?"

"Just like that." Xiao Qing closed her eyes. She reached into the deepest well of her memory, where she had kept Bai Suyin's final words locked away, polished and preserved, a shrine she visited every night for five centuries. "Take them."

She felt the Librarian's presence press against her mind. It was not painful, exactly. It was more like unwrapping a wound that had healed over, peeling back the scar tissue to reveal flesh that had never quite stopped bleeding.

*Qing'er.*

Her sister's voice. Warm. Terrified. Loving.

*Listen to me. You must run. Do not look back. Do not fight. You cannot win this, not yet. But you will. One day, you will be stronger than all of them. You will rise, and when you do, remember—*

The memory tore free.

Xiao Qing gasped. Her eyes flew open. She was on her knees, she realized. When had she fallen? Her chest felt hollow, empty, as if something vital had been carved out of her.

The Librarian held a small glass vial. Inside it swirled a pale green mist, glowing faintly. "It is done."

"The registry," Xiao Qing rasped.

"Follow me."

The vault was at the bottom of a spiral staircase that seemed to go on forever. Each step sent echoes spiraling upward, multiplying, until the sound became a chorus of footsteps that might have been her own or might have been the ghosts of everyone who had walked this path before.

The door at the bottom was made of bone. Human, Xiao Qing thought. Or something close enough.

The Librarian pressed her palm to the surface. The bone door groaned, shifted, and swung open.

Inside was a single scroll. It floated in the center of the chamber, surrounded by nine rings of golden light—the prohibitions. Each ring pulsed with celestial power, old and terrible.

"The original registry," the Librarian said. "You have one hour. After that, the prohibitions will reset, and anyone still inside will be... incorporated into the library."

Xiao Qing stepped forward. The rings of light did not stop her. They parted like curtains, recognizing that a price had been paid, that she had earned the right to pass.

She reached the scroll and unrolled it.

The writing was ancient. Older than the current celestial script, older than the dynasty that had rewritten history. But her sister had taught her to read this script, in the years before everything fell apart.

She found Bai Suyin's name.

And beneath it, she found the truth.

Her hands started shaking again, but this time it was not grief. It was fury.

The original registry listed Bai Suyin not as a criminal, not as a demon who had broken the laws of heaven, but as a *witness*. She had been there the night the old Jade Emperor died. She had seen who killed him. And she had refused to lie about it.

The current administration—the new Jade Emperor, the celestial court that now ruled the three realms—had not imprisoned her sister for loving a mortal. They had imprisoned her to silence her.

Xiao Qing read the entire entry. Then she read it again.

When she looked up, her eyes were green fire.

"Fahai," she whispered. "He knew."

The Librarian stood at the door, watching. "He was there that night. He helped cover it up. The White Snake was his greatest sin, and his greatest shame. He could not kill her—she was too powerful, too connected—but he could bury her. Seal her away. Make her a legend of forbidden love while the truth rotted in the dark."

Xiao Qing rolled the scroll carefully, tucking it into her sleeve. "I need to find Fahai."

"He is dead."

"No." She shook her head. "He's not. I would have felt it. My sister would have felt it. He's hidden somewhere, waiting, watching. He knows I'm coming."

The Librarian's sharp teeth gleamed. "Then you have a problem, little snake. Because if Fahai is still alive, he knows you have the registry. He will have prepared for this. He will have set traps, summoned allies, fortified his position. You are walking into a war."

Xiao Qing stepped past her, out of the bone vault, up the spiral stairs.

"I am the war."

She emerged from the Library of Forgotten Things at dawn. The mountains were golden, the sky clear. She breathed deep, filling her lungs with air that was real, that was alive, that was *hers*.

The scroll sat heavy in her sleeve. The truth sat heavier in her heart.

She began walking south, toward the human world, toward the temple where it had all begun.

She had taken three steps when the pendant at her throat flared hot.

Xiao Qing froze. The jade was burning against her skin, pulsing with a light she had not seen in five hundred years.

A voice. Her sister's voice, clear and strong, as if Bai Suyin were standing right beside her.

*Qing'er. I'm here.*

Xiao Qing's breath caught. "Sister?"

*The seal is weakening. Something has changed. I don't know how long I can hold on.*

"Where are you? Tell me where—"

*Leifeng Pagoda. But you cannot come for me yet. There is something you must know. The one who helped Fahai seal me—the one who truly betrayed me—*

The pendant went cold.

"Sister? SISTER!"

Silence.

Xiao Qing stood on the mountain path, the wind whipping her hair across her face, the scroll pressed against her arm, the pendant dead and cold against her chest.

Her sister was alive. Awake. Speaking.

And someone had betrayed her.

She looked south, toward the distant shape of Leifeng Pagoda, visible now as a dark needle against the horizon.

Then she ran.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Price of Forgotten Things

The Library of Forgotten Things didn't want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking through forests that shifted when she wasn't looking, crossing rivers that flowed backward, climbing mountains that changed elevation with each step. The terrain resisted her, not with malice, but with the quiet stubbornness of something that had decided long ago that it preferred solitude.

She understood the feeling.

On the third night, she stopped in a valley where the moonlight fell in silver sheets and the grass grew in perfect concentric circles. She sat cross-legged in the center of the largest circle and closed her eyes.

"You're not going to make this easy, are you?" she murmured.

The wind answered with silence.

She reached into her sleeve and touched Elder Xu's bone fragment. It was warm now, pulsing with a faint rhythm like a second heartbeat. She'd been carrying it for three days, and in that time, she'd come to understand something about it.

This wasn't just a fragment of bone. It was a fragment of memory.

Elder Xu had been a librarian once, before the celestial court recruited him. He'd served in the Archives of Heavenly Harmony for three hundred years before being reassigned to the prison. His bone remembered the old ways of finding things.

She pressed it against her forehead.

The world dissolved.

She was standing in a corridor made of light. Books floated past her, their pages fluttering like wings. A young man with Elder Xu's face — before the scars, before the prison — walked beside her, carrying a stack of scrolls.

"The Library of Forgotten Things doesn't have a door," he said, his voice echoing as if from very far away. "It has a question. And the question changes for everyone."

"What's the question?" she asked, but the memory was already fading, the young man dissolving into golden dust.

She opened her eyes.

The valley was gone.

She was standing in darkness so complete that she couldn't see her own hands. The ground beneath her feet was smooth, like polished stone. The air was cold and dry and smelled of old paper and something metallic.

"You took a shortcut," a voice said. "That's rude."

Xiao Qing's hand went to her dagger. "Show yourself."

A light flickered to life in front of her — a single candle floating in the air, its flame burning green. Beneath it stood a figure no taller than her waist, wrapped in robes made of shadow and starlight. Its face was a smooth mask of white jade, featureless except for two vertical slits where eyes should have been.

"I am the Keeper of the Threshold," the figure said. "You've brought a key. Show it."

Xiao Qing held up Elder Xu's bone fragment.

The Keeper tilted its head. "A librarian's anchor bone. Clever. But anchors require weight. What did you sacrifice to reach this place?"

"I didn't sacrifice anything. I found it."

"No." The Keeper's voice hardened. "You misunderstand. The key is not what you found. The key is what you lost along the way. Every step toward this library requires a sacrifice. You've been walking for three days. What did you give up?"

Xiao Qing felt a chill run through her.

She checked her qi. Her cultivation base was intact. Her meridians were clear. But something was missing. She reached deeper, searching her internal landscape, and found—

Three memories. Erased.

She remembered walking through the forest. She remembered the rivers, the mountains, the valley. But there were gaps now. Moments where she knew something had been, but couldn't recall what.

"The library takes memories as toll," the Keeper said. "Three days of walking. Three memories lost. You didn't notice because they were small ones. A childhood meal. A song you once knew. The face of a stranger who smiled at you once."

Rage flared in Xiao Qing's chest. "You had no right—"

"Every right." The Keeper stepped closer, and the green candle cast long shadows across its featureless face. "You came seeking the Library of Forgotten Things. The library forgets. That is its nature. You cannot enter without leaving something behind."

Xiao Qing forced herself to breathe. To calm the serpent stirring beneath her skin.

"What's the question?" she demanded.

The Keeper stopped. For a long moment, it said nothing.

Then: "The question is this. What do you seek that has been forgotten by all except you?"

The answer came immediately. "My sister's true name."

The Keeper's head snapped up. The slits where its eyes should have been widened, and Xiao Qing caught a glimpse of something burning in the darkness behind them.

"Bai Suyin," the Keeper whispered. "You seek the White Snake's original registry."

"Yes."

"That registry was sealed by order of the Jade Emperor himself. It contains truths that could shatter the current order of the three realms. Do you understand what you're asking?"

"I understand that my sister was framed. I understand that the celestial court lied. I understand that the only record of what really happened is hidden in your library, and I will burn this entire place to ash if I have to."

The Keeper laughed — a dry, rustling sound like dead leaves scraping across stone.

"You green snakes," it said. "Always so dramatic."

The darkness around them rippled, and suddenly they were standing in a vast hall filled with shelves that stretched upward into infinity. Books of every size and color lined the walls, some glowing, some whispering, some completely dark. The ceiling was a swirling mass of stars and clouds.

"The Library of Forgotten Things," the Keeper said. "Every book here contains a memory that the universe has chosen to forget. Every scroll holds a truth that someone, somewhere, decided was too dangerous to remember."

It gestured, and a ladder slid across the shelves, stopping at a section so high that Xiao Qing could barely see it.

"The original registries are kept in the Unwritten Wing. But I should warn you — that wing has a guardian. And the guardian does not speak. It does not negotiate. It does not accept bribes or threats or pleas."

"What does it do?"

The Keeper turned to face her. Its jade mask had cracked, just slightly, and through the裂缝 she saw something ancient and terrible looking back.

"It judges."

Xiao Qing climbed the ladder for what felt like hours. The shelves blurred past her, filled with books that called out to her in voices she almost recognized. Her mother's voice. Her sister's. The voices of people she'd known and forgotten and lost.

She didn't listen.

At the top of the ladder, she found a door made of bone and silk. It had no handle, no hinges, no visible way to open it. On its surface, characters were written in a script so old that it predated the celestial court's founding.

"Truth is the first casualty of power," she read aloud.

The door swung open.

The Unwritten Wing was a small room, barely ten paces across. In its center stood a single pedestal, and on that pedestal rested a scroll made of what appeared to be jade-green silk. Beside the pedestal sat a figure wrapped entirely in white bandages, like a corpse prepared for burial.

The guardian.

Xiao Qing stepped forward, and the guardian rose.

It was tall — impossibly tall — and as it stood, the bandages unraveled, revealing not a body but a vortex of white light. The light had no form, no face, no features, but she felt it looking at her. Looking through her.

"You seek the truth," the light said. Its voice was not a voice. It was the sound of a thousand people speaking at once, layered over each other like a chorus of ghosts.

"I do."

"The truth has a price."

"Everything has a price. Name it."

The light pulsed, and Xiao Qing felt it touch her core. She felt it read her cultivation, her memories, her intentions. She felt it find every secret she'd ever kept, every lie she'd ever told, every shadow in her heart.

"You carry the bone of a dead librarian," the light said. "You carry the rage of a sister wronged. You carry the weight of five centuries of waiting. But you also carry something else. Something you haven't acknowledged."

"What?"

"The desire to become what your sister was."

Xiao Qing's breath caught.

"You don't want to save Bai Suyin," the light continued. "You want to *replace* her. You want to step into the space she left behind. You want to be the White Snake, the powerful one, the one the world fears and respects. You've told yourself it's about justice, about truth, about love. But deep down, where you don't let yourself look, it's about ambition."

"That's not true."

"Isn't it?"

The light reached into her chest — not physically, but spiritually — and pulled out a thread of green energy that glowed with her own essence. She felt it tear, felt something vital separate from her being.

"To pass this threshold, you must surrender the lie you tell yourself. You must admit that your love for your sister is tangled with jealousy. That your quest for justice is also a quest for power. That you are not pure, Xiao Qing. You have never been pure."

Tears burned in her eyes. "I know."

"Say it aloud."

"I know I'm not pure." Her voice cracked. "I know I've been angry. I know I've been jealous. I know that sometimes, when I think about my sister, I hate her for leaving me, for being better than me, for being the one everyone remembered while I was just the green snake who followed her around."

The words tasted like poison. Like ash. Like truth.

"I love her more than anything in the three realms," Xiao Qing continued, her voice growing stronger. "But I also resent her. And I've spent five hundred years trying to bury that resentment under layers of loyalty and duty. I've failed. I'm still failing. I'm a mess of contradictions, and I don't know how to fix it."

The light pulsed again. Then it withdrew.

"You have passed," it said.

The scroll on the pedestal floated into the air and drifted toward her. She caught it with trembling hands.

"The truth is in your hands now," the light said. "But truths have consequences. Once you read what's written here, you cannot unread it. Once you know, the celestial court will know that you know. Are you prepared for that war?"

Xiao Qing looked down at the scroll. It was warm against her fingers, pulsing with an energy that felt almost alive.

"I've been preparing for five hundred years."

She unrolled it.

The characters were written in the same ancient script as the door, but she could read them as if they were written in her native tongue. They told a story she'd never heard before. A story that began not with Bai Suyin's sealing, but with her birth.

And the first line made Xiao Qing's blood run cold.

*"Before she was the White Snake, before she was Bai Suyin, she was something else. She was the first. She was the beginning. And the celestial court did not seal her because she broke their laws. They sealed her because she knew their greatest secret."*

Xiao Qing's hands shook.

*"The Jade Emperor is not the original ruler of heaven. He is a usurper. And the White Snake possesses the proof."*

The scroll continued, but Xiao Qing stopped reading. Her mind was racing, pieces falling into place like a puzzle she hadn't known she was solving.

Fahai's obsession with her sister.
The celestial court's willingness to destroy the entire White Snake lineage.
The way older cultivators whispered Bai Suyin's name like a curse and a prayer.

It wasn't about the law. It was never about the law.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Library of Forgotten Things

The stream carried the last traces of blood downstream, disappearing into the darkness. Xiao Qing stayed on her knees a moment longer, watching her reflection stabilize. The woman staring back was not the same one who had entered the prison. That woman had believed in patience. This one understood ash.

She stood and walked.

The journey to the Library of Forgotten Things was not measured in distance. It was measured in surrender.

Every step required her to release something. The memory of Elder Xu's voice, telling her to remember. The weight of Bai Suyin's jade pendant, warm against her chest. The rage that had carried her through five centuries of solitude.

The Library existed in the spaces between spaces. To find it, she had to become empty.

Xiao Qing walked for three days without eating. Without sleeping. Without letting her cultivation replenish her strength. She walked until her legs trembled and her vision blurred at the edges. She walked until the world became a haze of green and gray and the distant memory of pain.

On the third night, the path ended.

She stood at the edge of a cliff. Below, nothing but mist. Above, nothing but stars. Behind her, the forest she'd emerged from. Before her, the void.

"Here," she whispered.

The key required a sacrifice.

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out Elder Xu's bone. It was still warm, still carrying the final echo of his cultivation. She held it in her palm and felt the weight of his life. His mistakes. His regrets. His small kindnesses.

She had planned to use it as a tool. To channel his remaining qi into her own. To make his death mean something beyond ash.

But sacrifice was not the same as consumption.

Xiao Qing closed her eyes. She remembered Elder Xu teaching her to shape water. His gnarled hands guiding hers. His gruff voice saying, "The water doesn't care about your anger. It only cares about your intention."

She opened her eyes. She threw the bone into the mist.

It fell. And fell. And then—light.

The mist parted. A staircase of clouds spiraled downward, each step solid and glowing. At the bottom, barely visible, a structure took shape. Pillars of obsidian. Walls of something that looked like frozen moonlight. A door that had no handle.

Xiao Qing stepped onto the first cloud stair.

It held.

She descended for what felt like hours. The temperature changed. The air grew thick with the smell of old paper, old ink, old secrets. The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found. But once you found it, it remembered you forever.

She reached the bottom.

The door was massive—tall enough for a giant, wide enough for a dragon. It was carved with symbols she didn't recognize. Not celestial script. Not mortal writing. Something older. Something that predated language itself.

She pressed her palm against the door.

Nothing.

She pushed.

Nothing.

She stepped back, thinking. The key required a sacrifice. But she'd already given the bone. Why wasn't—

"The key is not the bone," said a voice behind her. "The key is what the bone represents."

Xiao Qing spun.

A woman stood at the base of the cloud stairs. She was tall, with skin the color of aged ivory and hair that moved like smoke. Her robes were the gray of twilight, and her eyes—her eyes held galaxies.

Xiao Qing's cultivation screamed a warning. This woman was not mortal. Not celestial. Not demon.

She was something else entirely.

"Who are you?" Xiao Qing asked.

"I am the Librarian." The woman smiled, and the smile did not reach her eyes. "You've been expected, Qing'er. For a very long time."

Xiao Qing's blood ran cold. No one called her that. No one except—

"You knew my sister."

"I knew many things." The Librarian walked past Xiao Qing, her feet not quite touching the ground. She stopped before the massive door and traced one of the symbols with her finger. "Bai Suyin came here, once. Before she met the mortal. Before she was sealed."

Xiao Qing's throat tightened. "She came here?"

"Looking for answers. Looking for a way to be human." The Librarian's voice softened, just slightly. "I told her she was already human. She didn't believe me."

"What did she find?"

"Nothing. Everything. The truth she wasn't ready to hear." The Librarian turned to face Xiao Qing. "You won't find what you're looking for inside that door."

"You don't know what I'm looking for."

"I know exactly what you're looking for." The Librarian stepped closer. Close enough that Xiao Qing could smell her—like ozone and old parchment. "You want the original registry. The one that predates the celestial court's rewrite. The one that holds the truth about your sister's crime."

Xiao Qing didn't flinch. "Yes."

"It's not in the Library."

"Liar."

The Librarian's eyes flashed. For a moment, the galaxies in them spun faster. "I do not lie. It's against my nature. The original registry was destroyed. Burned by the celestial court five hundred years ago, after your sister's sealing."

Xiao Qing's heart stopped. "No."

"They couldn't have a record that contradicted their version of events. They couldn't have proof that Bai Suyin was innocent." The Librarian's voice was calm, clinical. "So they burned it. Every copy. Every scrap. Every memory of its existence."

Xiao Qing's hands formed fists. Her cultivation surged. The pressure in the space between worlds grew heavy. "Then I have nothing."

"You have more than you know."

"Tell me."

The Librarian studied her for a long moment. Then she reached into her robe and pulled out a small scroll. It was yellowed with age, tied with a red ribbon that had faded to pink.

"This is not the registry," she said. "But it's a map. A map to the person who wrote it."

Xiao Qing stared at the scroll. "Who?"

"The last living witness to your sister's trial. The one who transcribed the original judgment before it was altered." The Librarian held out the scroll. "He's been in hiding for five centuries. Waiting for someone to find him."

"Why hasn't he come forward?"

"Because he's terrified. Because the celestial court would kill him. Because he has a family, descendants, a bloodline that would be erased if anyone discovered what he knows." The Librarian's eyes locked onto Xiao Qing's. "And because he's the one who burned the original registry."

Xiao Qing's hand stopped halfway to the scroll.

"He was the one who destroyed the evidence?"

"He was ordered to. He was a low-ranking celestial scribe. Refusal meant death." The Librarian's voice dropped. "But he was clever. He kept a copy. Not of the registry—but of his memory. A transcription of every word, every detail, written in a cipher only he can decode."

"Where is he?"

The Librarian pressed the scroll into Xiao Qing's palm. "The map will show you. But I warn you—he's not a good man. He's spent five hundred years running, five hundred years looking over his shoulder. He's paranoid. Dangerous. He'll kill you before he lets you near his secret."

Xiao Qing unrolled the scroll. It was blank.

"I don't see—"

"You will." The Librarian stepped back. "When you're ready. When you've accepted what you'll have to do to get the truth."

"And what's that?"

The Librarian's smile was sad. "The same thing you've always had to do. Become what they fear."

She turned and walked toward the cloud stairs.

"Wait," Xiao Qing called. "Why are you helping me?"

The Librarian paused. She didn't turn around. "Because I watched Bai Suyin fall. I watched her sacrifice everything for love." Her voice cracked, just slightly. "And I watched the celestial court rewrite her story. I've been waiting for someone to come and demand the truth."

"Then why not give me the registry directly?"

The Librarian finally turned. Her eyes were wet. "Because I couldn't save it. I tried. I reached for it as the flames consumed it. But I was too late." She looked at her hands. "I've been carrying this guilt for five hundred years. Let me carry it a little longer."

She ascended the stairs. The mist closed behind her.

Xiao Qing stood alone before the massive door, the blank scroll in her hands. She could feel the weight of the Library pressing against her cultivation. It wanted her to enter. It wanted her to explore its endless corridors of forgotten knowledge.

But the registry wasn't inside.

The truth was somewhere else.

She tucked the scroll into her sleeve and turned away from the door. As she did, she heard something—a whisper from the other side.

*Qing'er.*

She froze.

*Qing'er, don't go.*

It was Bai Suyin's voice.

Xiao Qing's hand pressed against the door. "Sister?"

*I'm here. I've always been here.*

The door began to glow. The symbols shifted, rearranging themselves into words she could read:

*THE PRISONER IN THE PAGODA IS NOT YOUR SISTER.*

Xiao Qing's breath caught.

*THE ONE YOU SEEK IS ELSEWHERE.*

*COME INSIDE.*

*I WILL SHOW YOU.*

The door groaned. It began to open, just a crack.

Xiao Qing's heart pounded. The Librarian had said the registry was destroyed. Had said the truth was outside. But this was Bai Suyin's voice. She knew it. She'd know it anywhere.

But something held her back.

Elder Xu's last words: *Remember who you are.*

The Librarian's warning: *He'll kill you before he lets you near his secret.*

The scroll in her sleeve, blank and waiting.

Xiao Qing stepped back from the door.

"I can't," she whispered. "Not yet."

The door stopped opening. The glow faded. The symbols returned to their unreadable forms.

Bai Suyin's voice—if it had ever been Bai Suyin's voice—fell silent.

Xiao Qing turned and ran up the cloud stairs.

---

She emerged from the mist in a different place. Not the forest. Not the cliff. A temple courtyard, overgrown with vines, the stones cracked and worn. The air smelled of incense and decay.

The scroll in her sleeve was no longer blank.

A map had appeared, drawn in ink that seemed to move. A path leading through mountains, across a sea, to a small island shaped like a crescent moon.

At the bottom, words had been written in a hand she didn't recognize:

*THE SCRIBE HIDES IN THE PLACE WHERE THE MOON NEVER SETS.*

*BRING HIM THE EYE OF THE STORM.*

*ONLY THEN WILL HE SPEAK.*

Xiao Qing looked up at the sky. The stars were unfamiliar. She was far from home.

But she was closer to the truth than she'd ever been.

She folded the scroll and began walking.

Behind her, in the Library of Forgotten Things, the massive door stood open. And something stepped through.

title: The Weight of Forgotten Things
content:
The Library of Forgotten Things did not welcome visitors.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, then two more climbing through mists that shifted like living things. The path to the library wasn't a path at all—it was a negotiation. Every step required her to convince the mountain that she was worthy of passage. Every breath tasted of old paper and dried herbs.

She found the entrance at midnight, hidden behind a waterfall that fell upward.

The water rose from a pool, climbed the cliff face, and vanished into clouds. Xiao Qing stood at the edge, feeling the pull of reversed gravity, the strange wrongness of water defying nature. She'd seen many impossible things in five centuries. This one made her skin crawl.

"To enter," a voice said, "you must give something you cannot reclaim."

Xiao Qing turned. An old woman sat on a rock that hadn't been there a moment ago. She was dressed in robes of woven shadow, her face a maze of wrinkles, her eyes the pale blue of winter sky. She held a bronze scale in her lap, perfectly balanced.

"I know the price," Xiao Qing said.

"Do you?" The old woman tilted her head. "You've heard stories. Rumors. Whispers from drunken cultivators in teahouses. But do you *know*?"

Xiao Qing reached into her sleeve and pulled out Elder Xu's bone. It glowed faintly in the moonlight, residual qi pulsing like a heartbeat. "I brought a sacrifice."

The old woman's eyes widened, just slightly. "That's not a sacrifice. That's a relic. Where did you—" She stopped, studying Xiao Qing with new interest. "Ah. The green snake. The one who burned the prison."

"I didn't burn it. I just—"

"You killed a celestial elder. Opened a restricted cell. Stole evidence." The old woman smiled, and her teeth were too sharp. "The celestial court has put a bounty on your head. Forty thousand contribution points. Nine immortal-grade treasures. A personal favor from the Jade Emperor himself."

Xiao Qing's stomach dropped. Forty thousand points. That was enough to buy a minor godhood. Enough to convince half the cultivators in the three realms to hunt her.

"I'm flattered," she said, keeping her voice flat.

"Don't be. They want you alive. The favor is for *delivery*, not disposal." The old woman laughed, a dry rasping sound. "You're worth more breathing than dead. That's rare."

Xiao Qing held up the bone. "Does this buy me entry, or do we negotiate further?"

The old woman examined her nails, which were long and curved like claws. "The bone of a celestial elder who died in service to the court? Yes, that will open the door. But the library doesn't just accept payment. It demands *purpose*. What are you seeking?"

"The original registry of demon spirits. The one from before the celestial reorganization."

For the first time, the old woman's expression shifted into something real. Surprise. Then fear. "That registry hasn't been touched in eight hundred years. It's sealed behind nine locks, each guarded by a manifestation of forgotten history. No one has even tried to access it since—" She stopped.

"Since what?"

"Since the last person who asked about it was erased from existence. Not killed. *Erased*. Their name, their deeds, their very impact on the world—gone. As if they'd never been born."

Xiao Qing's hand tightened around the bone. "I'll take that risk."

"You don't understand." The old woman stood, and the shadows around her writhed. "The library doesn't just contain knowledge. It *remembers* everything. Every truth, every lie, every secret that people have tried to bury. And some truths are buried because they're dangerous. The registry you seek—it doesn't just list demon spirits. It lists the *original* classifications. The ones that existed before the celestial court rewrote history to suit their narrative."

"I know."

"Do you know what you'll find? What they've hidden?"

Xiao Qing met her gaze. "I know my sister was not what they said she was. I know the records were falsified. I know Fahai didn't act alone."

The old woman was silent for a long moment. Then she stepped aside. "Place the bone on the scale."

Xiao Qing approached. The bronze scale sat perfectly level, waiting. She laid Elder Xu's bone on the left plate. The scale dipped, then rose again as something invisible settled on the right plate.

The old woman closed her eyes. "The library accepts your offering. Enter, green snake. But remember—the library does not forget. And it does not forgive."

She snapped her fingers.

The waterfall shifted. The water that had been falling upward now fell down, crashing into the pool with thunderous force. Behind it, a door appeared—carved from bone and jade, covered in writing so ancient it hurt to look at.

The door swung open.

Xiao Qing walked forward, but the old woman caught her arm. Her grip was iron.

"One more thing," she whispered. "There is someone else in the library. A visitor. She arrived three days ago, and she has not left the section you're seeking."

"Who?"

The old woman's smile was terrible. "You'll know her when you see her. She has been waiting for you."

She released Xiao Qing's arm and vanished, rock and shadow and all.

Xiao Qing stood at the threshold. The air from inside was cold and dry, smelling of dust and forgotten magic. She could feel the weight of the library pressing against her consciousness—millions of volumes, billions of secrets, all waiting to be unearthed.

She stepped inside.

---

The Library of Forgotten Things was not a building. It was a universe.

Xiao Qing stood on a platform of floating stone, surrounded by endless shelves that stretched in all directions—up, down, sideways, diagonally. Books drifted past like lazy fish. Scrolls unrolled themselves, read their own contents, then rolled back up. The light came from nowhere and everywhere, a soft amber glow that made everything look ancient.

A path of floating stones led forward. She took it.

The library spoke to her as she walked—not in words, but in impressions. She felt the weight of histories she hadn't lived, the echo of voices long silent, the ache of truths that wanted to be told. Every step stirred up fragments of memory that weren't hers.

*A woman crying in a burning temple.*

*A child holding a jade pendant.*

*A white snake coiled around a pagoda, waiting.*

She shook her head, clearing the visions. The registry. She needed the registry.

The path wound through sections she couldn't name—books bound in skin, scrolls written in blood, tablets carved from bone. She passed a section where the books whispered her name. She passed another where the shelves were empty, but the space between them hummed with suppressed knowledge.

And then she reached the center.

A vast circular chamber, domed like a temple. In the middle stood a pillar of crystal, and inside the crystal, floating in suspended amber light, was a book. Thick. Bound in what looked like black jade. Its cover was blank.

But it wasn't the book that made Xiao Qing stop.

It was the woman standing in front of it.

She was tall, dressed in robes of pale gold that seemed to absorb light. Her hair was silver-white, pulled back in an elaborate knot. Her face was beautiful in a way that felt wrong—too symmetrical, too perfect, like a mask carved by someone who had never seen a real face.

She turned when Xiao Qing entered.

Green eyes. The same shade as Xiao Qing's own.

"Hello, little sister," the woman said. Her voice was soft, melodic, and utterly cold. "I was wondering when you'd arrive."

Xiao Qing's hand went to her sword. "Who are you?"

The woman smiled. It didn't reach her eyes. "You don't recognize me? I'm wounded." She stepped closer, and the air around her crackled with power—ancient power, the kind that came from being old when the world was young. "My name is Su Qing. And I am your sister's true sister."

Xiao Qing's blood turned to ice.

"That's impossible. Bai Suyin only had one sister. Me."

"Is that what she told you?" Su Qing tilted her head, studying Xiao Qing like a curious specimen. "Or is that what the records say? The records you've come here to read?" She gestured at the crystal pillar. "Go ahead. Open the book. See for yourself what was erased."

Xiao Qing didn't move. "You're lying."

"Am I?" Su Qing's smile widened. "Why do you think the celestial court rewrote the original registry? Why do you think they sealed it away? Because the truth is inconvenient." She stepped closer, close enough that Xiao Qing could smell her—jasmine and copper, the same scent that clung to the prison cell. "Bai Suyin had two sisters, little green snake. A white snake and a green snake. But one of them was erased. Removed from history. *Forgotten*."

"And which one was that?"

Su Qing laughed. It was a beautiful sound, like wind chimes in a storm. "You're standing in front of her."

The crystal pillar pulsed. The book inside it glowed, responding to Su Qing's presence. Words began to appear on its blank cover, written in fire.

*Original Registry of Demon Spirits, Volume I: Serpent Clan.*

*Entry 1: Bai Suyin, White Serpent of Emei, Cultivation: Celestial Demon.*

*Entry 2: Su Qing, Green Serpent of Kunlun, Cultivation: Primordial Demon.*

*Entry 3: Xiao Qing, Green Serpent of Emei, Cultivation: None. Status: Created.*

Xiao Qing stared at the last word. *Created.*

"What does that mean?" she whispered.

Su Qing's expression softened, almost pitying. "It means you weren't born, little sister. You were *made*. A copy. A shadow. A replacement for the sister who was erased." She reached out and touched Xiao Qing's cheek. Her fingers were cold. "You're not real. You never were."

Xiao Qing's vision blurred. The world tilted. She could hear her heartbeat, too loud, too fast.

*Created.*

*Not real.*

*Never were.*

"No," she said, but her voice was weak. "That's not true. I remember. I remember growing up with her. I remember Emei. I remember—"

"What do you remember?" Su Qing interrupted. "Really remember? Not the memories that were implanted, but the ones that are truly yours?"

Xiao Qing opened her mouth. Closed it.

She tried to think of her earliest memory. The warm sun. The smell of peach blossoms. A white snake coiled around her, humming a lullaby.

But whose voice was humming? Whose arms were around her?

She couldn't remember her mother's face. Couldn't remember her own reflection before she was a thousand years old. Couldn't remember—

*Nothing.*

There was nothing before her sister. No family. No origin. No birth.

Just *created*.

Su Qing stepped back, satisfied. "You see now. You're a vessel. A vessel made to carry a love that belonged to someone else." She turned toward the crystal pillar, reaching for the book. "And I've been waiting a very long time to reclaim what was stolen from me."

She pulled the book from the crystal.

The library screamed.

The walls cracked. The shelves shook. Books fell like rain, their pages tearing loose, swirling into a storm of paper and ink. The dome above them

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Price of Memory

The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, following the thread of old stories, half-remembered songs, and the fading qi trails of cultivators who had sought the library before her. The trail led through mortal villages, across rivers that changed course when she tried to cross them, and finally into a forest that existed in the space between breaths.

The trees here had no shadows. The birds sang songs that hadn't been composed yet. And the air tasted like copper and honey.

She stopped at the edge of a clearing where the ground was perfectly circular, the grass trimmed to exact uniformity, and a single stone table sat in the center. On the table, a chess board. On the board, a game mid-play.

"Finally," a voice said. "I was beginning to think you'd lost your nerve."

The speaker sat cross-legged on the grass, ancient and ageless at once. They wore grey robes that seemed to absorb light, and their face was smooth as a river stone—no features except for two eyes that glowed like embers.

"You're the Keeper," Xiao Qing said. Not a question.

"I am the door," the Keeper replied. "The threshold. The question you must answer before you may enter." They gestured at the chess board. "Play me."

"I don't have time for games."

"You have exactly as much time as I give you." The Keeper's ember eyes flickered. "And I promise you, green snake, if you try to force your way past me, I will scatter your consciousness across seventeen dimensions and reassemble it as a tea cup. Sit."

Xiao Qing sat.

The pieces on the board were not jade or ivory or bone. They were memories—she could see them shifting, shimmering with the light of moments captured and preserved. Each piece held a story. Each move would cost her something.

"White moves first," the Keeper said.

"I don't know how to play."

"Everyone knows how to play. They just don't know they know." The Keeper's fingers hovered over the board, and a pawn—a memory of a child laughing—slid forward. "You learn by doing. And by losing."

Xiao Qing studied the board. The pieces watched her back. She reached out, touching a memory of her own—a fragment she hadn't known she possessed. A courtyard in spring. Her sister's voice, singing. She moved it forward.

The Keeper nodded. "Good. You learn fast."

They played in silence. Each move cost Xiao Qing a piece of herself, but she didn't know what she was losing until after she'd let it go. A taste faded from her tongue. The memory of her mother's face grew hazy. The name of a flower she'd loved vanished from her mind.

She was bleeding onto the board, and the Keeper was drinking her dry.

"Your sister," the Keeper said, not looking up from the game. "You seek proof of her innocence."

"I know she was innocent."

"Knowing and proving are different things." A knight moved—the memory of a soldier dying in battle. "The celestial court does not deal in truth. It deals in records. And records can be rewritten, erased, or simply... forgotten."

"My sister's record was destroyed."

"Was it?" The Keeper's ember eyes glowed brighter. "Or was it moved? Hidden? Placed somewhere that only the brave or the foolish would dare to seek it?"

Xiao Qing's hand paused over the board. "Tell me where it is."

"Tell me what you'll give me for that information."

"Anything."

The Keeper laughed—a sound like stones grinding together. "Everyone says that. No one means it." They gestured at the board. "You're losing, by the way. Three more moves, and I'll have your queen. And your memories of her."

Xiao Qing looked at the board. The Keeper was right. She'd been so focused on the conversation that she'd let herself be cornered. Her pieces—her memories—were scattered, vulnerable.

She'd spent five centuries learning to be patient. Learning to wait. Learning to sacrifice.

But she'd also spent five centuries learning to strike.

She moved her bishop—the memory of her sister's hand in hers, the day they'd fled the mortal village—and took the Keeper's rook.

The Keeper's eyes widened. "You—"

"Did you think I was just playing?" Xiao Qing moved again, taking a knight. "Did you think I didn't know what I was doing?"

"You're sacrificing your strongest memories!"

"I'm sacrificing the ones you wanted." She took another piece—the memory of her first transformation, the pain and joy of scales breaking through skin. "You've been reading me since we started. You know what I value. You've been steering me toward the pieces you wanted to take."

The Keeper's face—smooth, featureless—somehow managed to look impressed. "You let me."

"I let you think you were winning." Xiao Qing advanced her queen. "I know how this game works now. Every piece I lose, you gain. But every piece you lose—" She took the Keeper's queen. "—I don't get back."

The board shifted. The Keeper's pieces flickered, and for a moment, Xiao Qing saw them clearly—the memories the Keeper had taken from other seekers. Thousands of them. Millennia of stolen moments, waiting to be played.

"You're a parasite," she said.

"I'm a librarian." The Keeper's voice was cold. "Knowledge must be preserved. Memories must be kept safe. You think I enjoy taking them? I am the door, and the door must be paid."

"Then I'll pay." Xiao Qing moved her last piece—something she hadn't known she possessed until this moment. A memory so deep, so old, that it predated her conscious mind. The feeling of warmth. Of safety. Of being held.

She sacrificed it.

The board went dark.

The Keeper stared at the empty space where the memory had been. When they spoke, their voice was different. Softer. Almost human.

"Your mother," they said. "You just gave me your mother."

"She died when I was three days old. I don't remember her."

"You did. You had that memory buried so deep even you didn't know it was there." The Keeper's hands trembled. "No one has ever given me something so precious."

"Then open the door."

The Keeper stood. The chess board vanished, and the ground beneath it split open, revealing a staircase that spiraled downward into darkness. The air that rose from the depths smelled of paper and rot and ancient magic.

"The Library of Forgotten Things awaits," the Keeper said. "But know this, green snake. What you seek may not be what you find. The truth about your sister's registry—about her fate—it is not a gentle truth."

"Nothing about this has been gentle."

"Then go. And remember—" The Keeper held up her mother's memory, glowing faintly in their palm. "—I have this now. If you die in the library, it dies with you."

Xiao Qing walked to the edge of the staircase. The darkness below pulsed with a rhythm that felt almost like a heartbeat.

She didn't look back.

---
