Xiao Qing's eyes moved down the scroll. Past the evidence. Past the testimony from witnesses she didn't recognize—other serpents, mountain spirits, even a dragon who had spoken on Bai Suyin's behalf.

And then she found it.

The last entry. Written in a different hand, in a different ink—one that burned with golden light, the mark of a celestial judge.

*The petitioner Bai Suyin is found innocent of all charges. However, due to political considerations involving the Western Pure Land and the mortal monk Fahai, this verdict shall be sealed. The petitioner shall be publicly convicted of crimes she did not commit and sealed under Leifeng Pagoda for a term of no less than one thousand years.*

*Ordered by: The Jade Emperor.*

Xiao Qing's knees hit the floor.

She stared at the words until they blurred. The Jade Emperor. The highest authority in the celestial court. He had known Bai Suyin was innocent. He had known, and he had condemned her anyway—because it was politically convenient. Because Fahai had demanded it. Because the Western Pure Land had made threats.

Because a white snake's life was less important than celestial politics.

"Five hundred years," Xiao Qing breathed. "She's been suffering for five hundred years, and she never did anything wrong."

"Correct," the Librarian said. "The current administration has spent five centuries erasing this record. The registry you hold is the only surviving copy."

Xiao Qing's hands were shaking. The rage she'd kept contained for so long, the rage she'd cultivated into patience and stillness and calm—it cracked.

"Who else knows?"

"Very few. Those who knew are dead. Or sealed. Or silenced." The Librarian paused. "Except one."

Xiao Qing looked up. "Who?"

"The one who wrote the false verdict. The one who signed it with the Jade Emperor's seal." The Librarian's text-eyes flickered. "The celestial judge who condemned your sister."

"Name."

"Judge Zhao. He still serves on the celestial court. He has been promoted twice since Bai Suyin's trial."

Xiao Qing stood. The scroll rolled itself back up, and she took it, tucking it into her sleeve beside Elder Xu's bone. She looked at the Librarian—at the creature of stories and silence, the thing that had taken an hour of her future for this truth.

"I need to get to the celestial court."

"That would be suicide. Especially now." The Librarian tilted her head. "The court knows you escaped. They know you're hunting for the truth. They will be waiting."

"Then I'll find another way."

"There is no other way."

Xiao Qing smiled. It was not a pleasant smile. It was the smile of a green snake who had nothing left to lose.

"There's always another way."

She turned and walked out of the room, the scroll burning against her chest.

---

The celestial court convened in the Hall of Golden Light, a floating palace above the clouds where the laws

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Gate of Sacrifice

Xiao Qing walked for three days.

The forest of Kunlun gave way to barren plains, then to a desert of white sand that glittered like crushed bone under the moon. She didn't stop. She couldn't stop. Every moment she paused, she saw Elder Xu's face in the flames. Every time she closed her eyes, she heard the chains rattle.

The jade pendant pressed against her chest, warm and constant. It had guided her south, away from the celestial patrols, away from the main roads, deeper into the wilderness where even the demons feared to tread.

On the fourth night, the pendant went cold.

Xiao Qing stopped mid-stride. She pulled it from beneath her robes, watching the green light flicker once, twice, then die entirely. The stillness of the desert pressed around her, heavier than before.

"No," she whispered. "Not now. I'm so close."

She'd felt it—the thinness of the veil between realms, the way the air shimmered at the edge of her vision. The Library of Forgotten Things was near. She could almost taste it, like ozone and old paper.

But the key required a sacrifice.

Elder Xu's bone was still tucked in her sleeve. She'd hoped it would be enough—a piece of an immortal who'd guarded the celestial prisons for three thousand years. But as the pendant went dark, she understood.

The Library didn't want a piece of someone else's power.

It wanted hers.

Xiao Qing stood in the empty desert, the wind howling around her, and laughed. It was a bitter sound, cracked and worn thin.

"Five hundred years of cultivation," she said to the stars. "Five hundred years of building my foundation, honing my qi, becoming something that might stand against the heavens. And now I have to give it away."

She thought of Bai Suyin. Of her sister's laugh, soft and warm, like summer rain. Of the way she'd held Xiao Qing when the world was too loud, too bright, too cruel.

"Fine."

Xiao Qing sat down in the sand. She crossed her legs, placed her hands on her knees, and closed her eyes. The wind died. The stars seemed to lean closer, watching.

She reached inside herself and found her core.

It was beautiful—a spiral of green and gold, spinning slow and steady, pulsing with five centuries of accumulated power. Every breath she'd taken, every lesson she'd learned, every drop of blood she'd shed had fed this core. It was her. Not just her cultivation. Her soul.

She began to pull it apart.

The pain was immediate. It wasn't physical—it was deeper, the kind of agony that makes you forget your own name, that strips away everything you thought you were until only raw existence remains. Xiao Qing's hands clenched. Her teeth ground together. Blood dripped from her nose, her ears, her eyes.

She tore a thread of qi from the core and held it up.

"I offer this," she whispered. "A piece of my cultivation. Five decades of my life. A sacrifice of self."

The desert waited.

Then the sand began to shift.

It moved like water, flowing away from her in concentric circles, revealing stone beneath. Ancient stone, carved with symbols she'd never seen but somehow understood. They pulsed with a light that was older than the celestial court, older than the three realms, older than memory itself.

The ground opened.

Xiao Qing fell.

---

She landed hard on a floor of polished jade. The Library of Forgotten Things rose around her in impossible geometry—shelves that curved upward into darkness, books that floated like birds, corridors that turned in directions that shouldn't exist. The air smelled of dust and secrets.

And someone was waiting for her.

"Ah," said a voice like honey and broken glass. "I wondered when you'd find your way here, little snake."

Xiao Qing forced herself to stand. Her body felt hollow, diminished. She'd given up fifty years of cultivation, and she felt every year of it. But her eyes were sharp as she faced the figure before her.

She was a woman—or she wore the shape of one. Tall, with hair that shifted between colors like oil on water, and eyes that held galaxies. She wore robes of shadow and starlight, and she smiled with too many teeth.

"I know you," Xiao Qing said slowly. "You're the Librarian."

"I am." The Librarian inclined her head. "And you're the green snake who's been causing quite the disturbance in the celestial realm. Fahai is furious, you know. He's been pacing his temple, muttering about 'that wretched serpent.' It's rather entertaining."

"I need the original registry. The one that predates the current administration."

The Librarian's smile didn't waver, but something in her eyes sharpened. "That registry is not for mortal eyes. Not even for immortal ones."

"I'm neither," Xiao Qing said. "I'm a demon who's spent five centuries learning that the truth is the only thing worth dying for."

"Ah. But are you willing to kill for it?"

The question hung in the air. Xiao Qing's hand went to her sleeve, where Elder Xu's bone rested. She didn't answer.

The Librarian laughed. "Oh, I like you. Come, then. But know this—the Library demands balance. You gave a piece of yourself to enter. If you want what you seek, you'll have to give something else."

"What?"

The Librarian's smile widened. "I haven't decided yet."

She turned and walked into the labyrinth of shelves. Xiao Qing followed.

---

They walked for what felt like hours. The Library shifted around them, corridors appearing and disappearing, books rearranging themselves as if alive. Xiao Qing kept her hand on the jade pendant, but it remained cold and silent.

"This place holds the truth of everything that has ever been written," the Librarian said as they walked. "Every treaty, every lie, every love letter, every curse. The celestial court thinks they control history. But history lives here, breathing and dreaming, beyond their reach."

"Then you know about my sister."

"I know many things." The Librarian paused at a shelf that seemed to stretch into infinity. "Bai Suyin. The White Snake. The one who loved a mortal and was punished for it. Except—"

"Except what?"

The Librarian turned, her galaxy-eyes gleaming. "Except that's not the whole truth, is it? The celestial court punished her for loving a mortal. But they don't punish every immortal who descends. They don't seal every demon who breaks the rules. Why her? Why so harshly?"

Xiao Qing's heart hammered. "I've asked myself that question for five hundred years."

"And now you'll get your answer." The Librarian reached into the shelf and pulled out a scroll. It was old—older than anything Xiao Qing had ever seen. The parchment was yellowed, cracked, held together by threads of gold that glowed faintly.

"This is the original registry," the Librarian said. "It contains the true record of Bai Suyin's crime. Read it, and you'll understand why the celestial court wanted her gone."

Xiao Qing reached for the scroll.

The Librarian pulled it back. "But first—the balance."

"What do you want?"

The Librarian considered her, head tilted. "I've been the Librarian for ten thousand years. I've watched civilizations rise and fall, watched gods die and demons be born. I've read every story that ever existed. And I've grown bored."

She stepped closer, and for a moment, her face flickered—showing something ancient and hungry beneath the beautiful mask.

"I want a story I haven't heard before. I want the truth of what happens next. So I'll give you the registry, and you'll let me watch. From beginning to end. Whatever path you choose, wherever this journey takes you—I'll be there. Invisible. Watching. Recording."

Xiao Qing's throat tightened. "You want to see my life."

"I want to see your story. There's a difference." The Librarian held out the scroll. "Agreed?"

The jade pendant pulsed once, warm against Xiao Qing's chest. A warning? A blessing? She couldn't tell.

She took the scroll.

"I agree."

The Librarian smiled—a real smile, this time, almost kind. "Then read. And learn the truth that the heavens tried to bury."

Xiao Qing unrolled the scroll.

The characters were ancient, written in a script that predated the current celestial language. But she'd studied. She'd prepared. She read.

And her world shattered.

---

The crime of Bai Suyin was not loving a mortal.

The crime of Bai Suyin was knowing the truth.

The scroll detailed a war—not the war between gods and demons that the history books recorded, but something older. Something the celestial court had tried to erase.

The original gods were not benevolent. They were tyrants who enslaved the mortal realm, feeding on human suffering. The current celestial court was born from a rebellion—a bloody, terrible rebellion that killed the old gods and installed new ones.

But some of the old gods survived. They went into hiding, waiting, plotting.

Bai Suyin discovered this. She discovered that the celestial court was not as stable as it appeared. She discovered that the seal on the old gods was weakening. And she discovered that the celestial court knew—and was doing nothing.

They were afraid.

So they silenced her.

They spun a story about a white snake who fell in love with a mortal, who broke the sacred laws, who deserved her punishment. And the three realms believed it.

But the truth was worse.

The truth was that Bai Suyin was not a criminal.

She was a warning.

And the celestial court had made an example of her to keep anyone else from digging too deep.

Xiao Qing lowered the scroll. Her hands were shaking. Her whole body was shaking.

"Sister," she whispered. "What did you find?"

The Librarian was watching her with those galaxy-eyes. "Now you know. The question is—what are you going to do with that knowledge?"

The Library trembled. Somewhere in the distance, an alarm began to sound.

"They've found me," Xiao Qing said.

"Not yet. But they will." The Librarian gestured, and the shelves began to shift, forming a path. "There's a way out. It leads to the mortal realm, far from celestial patrols. You can disappear, gather your strength, decide what to do next."

Xiao Qing looked at the scroll in her hands. Then at the path.

"What about you? Won't they know you helped me?"

The Librarian laughed. "I'm the keeper of all stories, little snake. I've helped everyone who's ever walked through my doors. The celestial court can't touch me. I know too many of their secrets."

She leaned in, her voice dropping to a whisper.

"But there's something you should know. The one who betrayed your sister—who told the celestial court what she'd discovered—is still alive. And they've been waiting for you."

"Who?"

The Librarian's smile was sharp as a blade. "The one who's been watching from the shadows. The one who smiled when you escaped the prison. The one who's been guiding you here, step by step, because they need you to finish what your sister started."

Xiao Qing's blood turned to ice. "You're saying—"

"I'm saying that your sister's story isn't over. It's just been waiting for someone to read the next page." The Librarian stepped back, her form beginning to dissolve into shadow. "And that someone is you."

The Library shook harder. Cracks appeared in the jade floor.

"Go," the Librarian said. "Before the celestial court seals this place too."

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Weight of Memory

The Library of Forgotten Things did not welcome visitors.

Xiao Qing had walked three days through the mountain pass, then another two through valleys where the shadows moved against the wind. She'd crossed a river that tasted of iron and a field of white flowers that released spores designed to induce sleep. She'd burned through a third of her remaining qi just to stay awake.

Now she stood at the edge of nowhere.

The records said the library existed between realms, accessible only through a specific resonance of blood and intent. The key required a sacrifice—but the texts never specified what kind. Elder Xu had mentioned it once, in passing, during one of their rare conversations about forbidden knowledge. She'd assumed he meant a physical sacrifice. Blood. Pain. Something measurable.

She was wrong.

The entrance revealed itself at midnight, when the moon hung directly overhead and the stars formed a pattern she'd never seen before. A door appeared in the empty air—not carved from wood or stone, but woven from threads of light that pulsed like a heartbeat.

Xiao Qing approached cautiously. The door had no handle. No lock. Only a surface that rippled when she breathed near it.

"You seek knowledge," said a voice. Not from behind her. Not from the door. From inside her own skull.

"I seek the truth," she replied.

"Same thing. Different price."

The surface of the door shifted, and she saw her reflection—but it wasn't her reflection. It was a younger Xiao Qing, the one who'd followed Bai Suyin through the streets of Hangzhou, the one who'd laughed at mortal festivals and stolen peaches from celestial orchards. The one who'd believed her sister could never fall.

"You want the original registry," the reflection said. "The one that predates the current administration. Do you know what that registry contains?"

"The truth about my sister's trial."

"More than that." The reflection smiled, and it was wrong—too wide, too knowing. "It contains the truth about why she was sealed. Not the official reason. The real one."

Xiao Qing's hands clenched. "Tell me."

"Come inside. Read it yourself. But understand the price."

"I've already paid in blood."

"Blood is cheap." The reflection's eyes turned black, bottomless. "Memory is not."

The door opened.

---

The Library of Forgotten Things had no walls.

Xiao Qing stood on a floor of polished obsidian that reflected a sky she didn't recognize—purple constellations, twin moons, a sun that burned green at the edges. Bookshelves stretched in every direction, impossibly tall, impossibly far. Some books glowed. Some whispered. Some bled.

A figure waited at the center. Old. Female. Wrapped in robes that looked like woven shadow.

"You're early," the figure said. "I didn't expect you for another century."

Xiao Qing stopped. "You know who I am."

"I know everything that enters my library." The figure turned, and her face was a blur—features shifting, never settling. "I am the Keeper. I have been here since before the first god woke, and I will be here after the last one dies. You want the original registry. It exists. I can give it to you."

"Name your price."

The Keeper tilted her head. "I told you already. Memory."

"Which memory?"

"Your strongest. Your most defining. The one that made you who you are." The Keeper stepped closer, and Xiao Qing felt her qi being read, weighed, judged. "For most people, I take a single memory. A birthday. A death. A first kiss. But you—" The Keeper's blurred face seemed to focus, just for a moment, on Xiao Qing's eyes. "You are not most people. For you, I will take the memory of your sister's face."

Xiao Qing's blood went cold. "No."

"Then you leave. The library closes to you forever. You find another way."

"There has to be another option."

"There is." The Keeper's voice dropped. "You can give me the memory of the day she was sealed. The moment you watched her disappear behind the golden chains. The sound she made when Fahai's spell took hold."

Xiao Qing's ribs ached. The wounds from the prison had healed, but the memory hadn't. It never did.

"That's worse," she said.

"I know." The Keeper wasn't cruel. She was simply honest. "But I don't take memories to be cruel. I take them because they feed the library. Every memory I collect becomes a book. A record that can never be lost, never be burned, never be forgotten. Your sister's registry is in this library because someone paid a similar price."

"Who?"

"A monk. Five hundred years ago. He gave me his memory of her face to ensure her true story would survive."

Xiao Qing's breath caught. "Fahai?"

"He was not always what he became." The Keeper's voice softened, almost imperceptibly. "He was young once. Idealistic. He believed he was doing the right thing. But doubt crept in. Doubt grows roots. He came here after the sealing, before the glory, before the temple. He asked me to preserve the truth."

"Why didn't he destroy the false records?"

"Because he was afraid." The Keeper spread her hands. "Fear makes people do strange things. He wanted the truth to exist, but he didn't want anyone to find it. So he hid it here, in the one place that requires a sacrifice to enter."

Xiao Qing stared at the endless shelves. Somewhere in this impossible space was the truth about her sister. A truth that Fahai himself had preserved.

"I need to see it," she said.

"Then choose."

---

Xiao Qing sat on the obsidian floor. The Keeper waited.

She thought about her sister's face. The curve of her smile. The way her eyes crinkled when she laughed. The way she looked when she was angry—a cold stillness that made even Xiao Qing afraid. The way she looked when she was proud, when she was sad, when she was tired.

Five hundred years. Five hundred years of holding that image in her mind, using it as fuel, as purpose, as the reason she kept breathing.

If she gave it up, what would she have left?

"Can I give you something else?" she asked. "A different memory. A different sacrifice."

"The library doesn't negotiate."

"I'm not negotiating. I'm asking if there's an alternative I haven't considered."

The Keeper was silent for a long moment. Then she said, "There is one. But it's more dangerous."

"Tell me."

"You can give me the memory of the moment you chose to become what you are. Not the moment you decided to cultivate—the moment you decided to become a weapon."

Xiao Qing's stomach turned.

She knew that memory. She'd buried it deep, locked it away, refused to acknowledge it even in her own thoughts. Because acknowledging it meant accepting what she'd done.

The day after Bai Suyin was sealed. The day Xiao Qing had crawled out of the ruins of their home, bleeding from a dozen wounds, her cultivation shattered, her spirit broken. She'd found a mortal village. She'd been too weak to hide her true form. The villagers had seen her—a green snake, coiled in pain, dying.

They'd tried to kill her.

She'd killed them first.

Not in self-defense. Not in desperation. She'd killed them because she was angry. Because her sister was gone. Because the world had taken everything from her and she wanted to take something back.

Twenty-three mortals. Men, women, children. She'd slaughtered them all.

Then she'd climbed the mountain and started cultivating with a single purpose: to become strong enough to never be weak again.

"That memory," the Keeper said, "is why you're dangerous. Not your cultivation. Not your blood. That choice. If you give it to me, you give up the part of you that can make that choice again."

Xiao Qing's hands were shaking. "I'd become... what? A different person?"

"You'd become someone who cannot hate. Someone who cannot kill in rage. Someone who can only act from love or duty." The Keeper's voice was gentle. "Is that who you want to be?"

She thought about Elder Xu. She thought about the celestial guards she'd killed to escape the prison. She thought about the blood on her hands, the way it had felt, the way she'd told herself it was necessary.

Was it?

Or had she just wanted to hurt them because they'd hurt her?

"I don't know," she whispered.

"Then you're not ready." The Keeper began to fade. "Come back when you've decided. The door will open again. Or it won't."

"Wait." Xiao Qing stood. "The monk. Fahai. What did he become after he gave up his memory?"

The Keeper paused. "He became certain. Without the memory of her face, he no longer saw her as a person. She became a symbol, a concept, a threat. He could seal her without hesitation because he'd forgotten that she had ever smiled at him."

Xiao Qing felt sick. "You're saying if I give up my memory of her, I'll become like him?"

"I'm saying memory is what connects us to others. Without it, we can only see the world through our own eyes." The Keeper's form solidified again. "But I'm also saying that some memories are too heavy to carry. They break us. They hollow us out. And sometimes, letting them go is the only way to survive."

"I don't want to survive," Xiao Qing said. "I want to save her."

"Then you have to decide what you're willing to lose."

The Keeper vanished.

Xiao Qing stood alone in the Library of Forgotten Things, surrounded by books that held the memories of everyone who had ever paid the price. Somewhere in these shelves was the true story of Bai Suyin's trial. Somewhere was the answer she'd been seeking for five centuries.

But to reach it, she had to let go of the one thing that had kept her going.

She sat down again. The obsidian floor was cold. The whispers of the books filled the air like distant conversations.

And for the first time in five hundred years, Xiao Qing allowed herself to truly consider what it would mean to forget.

---

She didn't know how long she sat there. Time moved differently in the library. Hours could be minutes. Years could be seconds.

A book fell from a shelf nearby. It landed at her feet, open to a page that glowed with golden light.

Xiao Qing looked down.

The page showed an image—two women standing on a bridge in Hangzhou. One in white. One in green. They were laughing. The sun was setting behind them. It looked like paradise.

Beneath the image, written in a hand she recognized, was a single line:

*"I remember this. I remember her. Before I forgot, I wanted someone to know she was happy once."*

Signed: *Fahai.*

Xiao Qing picked up the book. Her hands trembled. The pages were warm, as if they'd been waiting for her.

She read the entry. It described Fahai's final moments before the sealing—not as history recorded them, but as he'd experienced them. His doubt. His fear. The moment he'd looked at Bai Suyin and seen not a demon, but a woman.

And then the moment he'd closed his eyes and chosen to forget.

*"I could not both seal her and love her. So I chose to seal her. And I asked the library to take the love, so that I would not falter."*

Xiao Qing closed the book.

She understood now.

Fahai hadn't been

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Library's Price

The forest swallowed her whole.

Xiao Qing moved through the ancient pines like smoke, her feet barely touching the ground. Three days had passed since Elder Xu's death, three days of running, three days of feeling the jade pendant pulse against her chest like a second heart. The celestial guards had spread across the southern provinces, their qi signatures flaring like beacons in her awareness. She'd dodged three patrols already, each time by seconds.

She needed the Library of Forgotten Things. But finding it required more than just knowing where to look.

The old texts spoke of a guardian who tested all who sought entry. A being older than the celestial court itself, bound to the space between worlds. Some called it the Keeper. Others called it the Mirror. No one who failed the test ever spoke of what they saw.

Xiao Qing stopped at the edge of a cliff, the wind whipping her hair across her face. Below her, a valley stretched into mist, the trees so dense that no sunlight touched the ground. Somewhere in that darkness, a portal waited.

But she couldn't enter without the key.

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out Elder Xu's bone. It was warm, still humming with residual power. She'd hoped it would be enough. She'd hoped the sacrifice of a man who'd known her sister would satisfy the Keeper's hunger.

She was wrong.

"I need more," she said to the empty air. "What else can I give?"

The wind carried no answer. But the jade pendant grew hot against her skin, and she heard—or thought she heard—a voice, soft and familiar, whispering her name.

*Xiao Qing. Find me.*

Her sister's voice. Or the echo of it, preserved in the pendant's ancient magic. Either way, it was enough to make her continue.

She descended into the valley, her senses stretched to their limits. The trees here were wrong—their bark black, their leaves silver, their roots pulsing with a rhythm that matched no heartbeat she'd ever felt. The air grew heavy, thick with the scent of old magic and older sorrow.

The portal appeared without warning.

One moment, there was only darkness between two massive oaks. The next, a door stood there, carved from bone and jade, its surface inscribed with characters that predated the first dynasty. It hummed with power, with hunger, with the weight of countless souls who'd come seeking answers and never left.

The Keeper stood beside it.

She was tall—taller than any human, taller than most celestial beings—her body formed of shifting mist and broken mirrors. Her face was a kaleidoscope of expressions, none of them staying long enough to read. Her eyes were hollow sockets filled with starlight.

"You have come far, little serpent." The Keeper's voice was a chorus of a thousand voices, all speaking slightly out of sync. "You carry death in your sleeve and love in your heart. Which one do you offer?"

Xiao Qing stood her ground. "I need to enter the library."

"You need to pay the price." The Keeper tilted her head, and her face shifted into a perfect copy of Bai Suyin's. "Everything has a cost, little sister. You know this better than most."

The sight of her sister's face on that monstrous form made Xiao Qing's stomach turn. She forced herself to remain still, forced her voice to remain steady.

"What price?"

"Memory." The Keeper's form shifted again, becoming a young woman with silver hair and golden eyes. "Not yours. Theirs. The ones you've lost. The ones who've died for you. Their final moments—I want them. Every one."

Xiao Qing's hand went to the bone in her sleeve. "I have Elder Xu's last memory. Is that enough?"

"One memory for one entry. But you carry more than one death, don't you?" The Keeper stepped closer, and the mirrors on her body caught Xiao Qing's reflection, multiplied it, distorted it. "You carry the death of your mother, who drowned in the East Sea. You carry the death of your master, who was struck by heavenly lightning. You carry the death of the woman who raised you, who was torn apart by demon wolves."

"How do you—"

"I see everything that clings to you." The Keeper's hand, formed of mist and broken glass, reached out and touched Xiao Qing's cheek. "You are a walking graveyard, green snake. Every person who ever loved you is dead. Every person who ever taught you is ash. And you still have the audacity to hope."

Xiao Qing's jaw tightened. "Give me the memories. I'll pay."

"No." The Keeper withdrew her hand. "I don't want them from you. I want you to *relive* them. I want you to feel every death, every failure, every moment you couldn't save them. I want your suffering, not your permission."

The mirrors on the Keeper's body began to glow.

Xiao Qing tried to step back, but her feet were rooted to the ground. The trees around her warped, their branches reaching for her, their roots curling around her ankles. The jade pendant blazed against her chest, but even its power couldn't break this hold.

"I am not your enemy," the Keeper said, her voice softening. "I am your teacher. The library holds the truth you seek, but truth requires sacrifice. You will enter the memories of everyone you've failed. You will live their deaths. And when you emerge—if you emerge—you will know what it means to carry the weight of love."

"No." Xiao Qing's voice cracked. "I can't. I can't watch them die again."

"You can. You will." The Keeper's form dissolved into mist, and Xiao Qing's world shattered into a thousand fragments.

---

She was in the East Sea, watching her mother sink.

The water was cold, so cold that it burned. Her mother's face was calm, resigned, her white robes billowing around her like wings. She didn't fight. She didn't scream. She simply closed her eyes and let the darkness take her.

*Why?* Xiao Qing tried to scream, but she had no voice, no body, no power. She was just a witness, trapped in a memory that wasn't hers.

*Because she loved you,* a voice whispered. *Because the celestial court demanded a sacrifice, and she gave herself so you could live.*

The scene shifted.

She was on a mountain peak, watching her master burn. Heavenly lightning struck him again and again, each bolt tearing through his cultivation, his meridians, his soul. He didn't scream either. He looked at her—at the memory of her, a child Xiao Qing with tears streaming down her face—and he smiled.

*Remember what I taught you,* he said. *The water is patient. The water is strong. The water can carve mountains.*

The scene shifted again.

The woman who raised her. A mortal cultivator with kind eyes and calloused hands. Demon wolves surrounded her, their jaws dripping with blood. She held a staff made of peach wood, and she fought until her arms gave out, until her legs gave out, until she was nothing but a broken body in a pool of red.

*Live,* she mouthed. *Live for me.*

Xiao Qing screamed.

She screamed and screamed, but no sound escaped. She was drowning in memories, drowning in deaths, drowning in the weight of every person who'd ever loved her and died for it.

The Keeper's voice came through the chaos, calm and clinical. *You have carried these deaths for five hundred years. You have buried them, suppressed them, refused to mourn. But grief is not a burden to be carried—it is a river to be crossed. You must feel it. You must accept it. Or it will destroy you.*

"I can't," Xiao Qing sobbed. "I can't do this. I'm not strong enough."

*You are not the little sister anymore. You are not the green snake who followed in her elder's shadow.* The Keeper's voice softened. *You are the one who survived. You are the one who endured. You are the one who watched everyone she loved die and still chose to love again.*

The memories began to fade, dissolving like mist in sunlight.

Xiao Qing found herself on her knees, her body shaking, her face wet with tears. The portal stood before her, open. The Library of Forgotten Things stretched beyond it, endless shelves of scrolls and books and tablets, all glowing with ancient light.

The Keeper was gone.

But someone else was waiting for her at the library's entrance.

A woman. Tall, with silver hair that fell to her waist and golden eyes that held the weight of centuries. She wore robes of white and blue, and she smiled when she saw Xiao Qing.

"I've been waiting for you," the woman said. "I knew you'd come eventually."

Xiao Qing's heart stopped. Her breath caught in her throat. Her hands began to tremble.

Because the woman's face, her voice, her presence—they were all wrong. They were all impossible.

"You're dead," Xiao Qing whispered. "I saw you die. I *felt* you die."

The woman tilted her head, her smile never wavering. "Did you?"

And then she stepped forward, and the library's light caught her face, and Xiao Qing saw what she should have seen from the start.

The woman was not Bai Suyin.

But she wore her face. Her exact face. Every feature, every expression, every mannerism imprinted with impossible precision.

"Who are you?" Xiao Qing demanded, her voice rising. "Why do you look like my sister?"

The woman's smile turned sad. "Because I was created in her image. Because she asked me to wait for you." She extended her hand. "My name is Jingyi. And I have something that belongs to you."

From her sleeve, she pulled out a letter. Yellowed with age, sealed with white wax, bearing a single character in Bai Suyin's handwriting.

*Qing.*

"The day before she was sealed," Jingyi said, "she gave me this. She told me to find you when you were ready. She told me you would come to this library, seeking answers." Her golden eyes met Xiao Qing's. "She knew you would come. She knew everything."

Xiao Qing stared at the letter, her hands still trembling.

Five hundred years. Five hundred years of searching, of waiting, of grieving. And her sister had known. Had planned for this moment. Had left her a message, carried by a stranger who wore her face.

She reached out and took the letter.

The paper was warm, pulsing with familiar qi. The seal cracked under her fingers, and the characters inside began to glow.

*My dearest sister,*

*If you are reading this, then I am gone. But do not mourn me. Do not waste your tears on a fate I chose willingly.*

*I did not tell you everything. I could not. There are truths that cannot be spoken, only discovered. But I will tell you this: my sealing was not a defeat. It was a plan. A plan that has been in motion for a thousand years.*

*You are not the only one who has been searching for answers, Xiao Qing. I have been searching too. And what I found will shake the foundations of the three realms.*

*Come to me. Find the mountain that is not on any map. Find the temple that exists only in memory. I will be waiting.*

*Your loving sister,*

*Bai Suyin*

Xiao Qing looked up, her eyes burning.

"The mountain that is not on any map," she repeated. "The temple that exists only in memory."

Jingyi nodded. "I know where it is. I've been waiting to show you."

"Then take me

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER SIXTEEN: The Price of Memory

The Library of Forgotten Things did not welcome visitors.

Xiao Qing had spent three days searching for its entrance. Three days of crossing mountain ranges that shifted beneath her feet, of navigating rivers that flowed backward, of passing through villages where the people spoke in tongues she almost recognized but couldn't quite understand. The borderlands between realms were unstable, treacherous, and utterly indifferent to mortal suffering.

She found it at twilight on the fourth day.

Not a building. Not a cave. A single door standing in the middle of an empty field, its frame made of bone and its surface covered in writing that moved like living things. The characters squirmed, rearranged themselves, formed words that dissolved before she could read them.

Xiao Qing approached slowly. Her hand went to the jade pendant at her throat—still warm, still glowing faintly. The bone fragment from Elder Xu was tucked into her sleeve, a weight that reminded her of what she'd lost and what she'd sworn.

The door had no handle. No hinges. Just a smooth surface that pulsed with a rhythm like a heartbeat.

"You're early."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. Old. Female. Carrying the weight of centuries.

Xiao Qing didn't turn. She kept her eyes on the door. "I didn't know there was a schedule."

"There isn't." A pause. "But most who seek this place spend decades wandering. Years. You found it in days. That's... unusual."

"I'm an unusual snake."

A laugh, dry as autumn leaves. "That you are, little one. That you are."

The air rippled, and a figure stepped out from behind the door—except there was no behind. The woman had simply been hidden by the space itself, folded into a corner of reality that Xiao Qing's eyes couldn't perceive until she chose to be seen.

She was ancient. Not in the way of old cultivators who preserved their youth with qi, but truly ancient—wrinkles carved deep into her skin, white hair hanging to her waist, eyes that held the color of old tea. She wore robes that had once been blue but had faded to gray, patched in a hundred places with thread of different colors.

"Keeper of the Library?" Xiao Qing asked.

"Keeper. Guardian. Janitor." The old woman shrugged. "I've had many titles. None of them matter here. What matters is why you've come."

"I need information."

"Everyone needs information. That's what libraries are for." The old woman's eyes sharpened. "But this library is different. This library remembers what the world has chosen to forget. And forgetting, little snake, is usually a kindness."

"I'm not here for kindness."

"No. I can see that." The old woman circled her, moving with surprising grace. "You're here for rage. For grief. For a sister who was taken and a truth that was buried." She stopped in front of Xiao Qing. "The question is: can you bear what you find?"

Xiao Qing met her gaze. "I've borne five hundred years. I can bear more."

"Bold words." The old woman smiled, and there was something sad in it. "But the weight of memory is different from the weight of time. Time passes. Memory stays. It festers. It grows teeth."

"I have teeth of my own."

"Yes." The old woman reached out and touched the jade pendant. Her fingers barely brushed it, but Xiao Qing felt a shock run through her—not pain, but recognition. As if the pendant knew this woman. "Yes, you do. Come, then. But know this: the Library requires a price for entry. A memory. One that matters to you."

Xiao Qing's hand went to the pendant. "What kind of memory?"

"Not your choice. The Library chooses. It will take what it deems most valuable." The old woman's voice softened. "This is the only warning I will give you. The Library does not return what it takes. You will lose whatever it selects. Forever."

Xiao Qing thought of her sister's face. Of the day they'd played in the lotus pond, young and free and unaware of the tragedy that awaited them. Of the way Bai Suyin had laughed—clear and bright as morning light.

She thought of Fahai's golden bowl. Of the chains. Of Elder Xu burning to ash.

"I understand," she said.

The old woman studied her for a long moment. Then she nodded and stepped aside.

The door swung open.

Inside, there was no building. No shelves. No books.

There was a void. Infinite. Dark. Filled with whispers that brushed against Xiao Qing's mind like fingers.

"Step forward," the old woman said from behind her. "The Library will take what it needs. Then you may ask your question."

Xiao Qing stepped into the darkness.

The whispers grew louder. They became voices—thousands of them, millions, all speaking at once. She caught fragments of conversations, of prayers, of curses, of love declarations and deathbed confessions. History's detritus. The things the world had decided were not worth keeping.

But the Library kept them.

Something touched her. Not physically, but deeper. It reached into her memories, sifting through them like a hand through water. She felt it pass over moments of joy, of sorrow, of mundane days and extraordinary nights.

It paused.

Xiao Qing felt a sudden, terrible cold.

And then it took.

---

She was standing in the garden. The one behind the mountain temple where she'd trained for the first fifty years of her isolation. It was spring. The peach blossoms were falling, pink and white, carpeting the ground like snow.

Elder Xu was there. Younger. Less worn. He was teaching her to shape water.

"Don't force it," he said. "Water doesn't respond to force. It responds to patience. To understanding. You must become the water, Xiao Qing. Not control it—become it."

She was frustrated. She'd been trying for weeks and could barely make a ripple. "I don't understand."

"You will." He smiled. "When you stop trying to be what you're not, and start being what you are."

"What am I?"

He looked at her with those ancient eyes. "You are the one who will remember when everyone else forgets."

The memory dissolved. The garden faded. Elder Xu's face became indistinct, then gone entirely.

And Xiao Qing felt it—the loss. A hole where something precious had been. She could still remember the lesson. She could still remember his words. But the moment itself, the feeling of standing in the garden with the peach blossoms falling, the warmth of his presence beside her—that was gone.

Forever.

She gasped and opened her eyes.

She was standing in a room. A real room, with walls and shelves and books. Thousands of books, stretching up into darkness. The Library had given her access.

The old woman stood beside her, watching with something like pity.

"It took your first lesson with him," she said. "The one where he told you your purpose."

Xiao Qing's throat was tight. "I remember it."

"You remember the words. The meaning. But the experience—the color of the blossoms, the sound of his voice, the smell of the earth after rain—that is gone. The Library has it now."

Xiao Qing closed her eyes. Breathed. Let the anger rise and then settle.

"I can live with that."

"Can you?" The old woman tilted her head. "You've lost something irreplaceable, and you haven't even asked your question yet. Are you sure you want to continue?"

"I'm sure."

"Then ask. The Library will answer truthfully. But be specific. The less specific the question, the more general the answer, and you don't have time for riddles."

Xiao Qing opened her eyes. She looked at the shelves, at the books that held forgotten histories, at the darkness that stretched above her like a sky of paper and ink.

"What was written in the original registry about my sister Bai Suyin?"

The Library responded immediately.

A book flew off a shelf—not flying, but appearing in the air before her, pages fluttering open. It was old. Leather-bound, with characters that predated the current celestial dynasty. The ink was brown with age, the paper brittle.

Xiao Qing caught it. Her hands were shaking.

She read.

The first page was a description of Bai Suyin's birth—how she'd emerged from the White Jade Pool in the mountains of Emei, fully formed and radiant. The second page detailed her early cultivation, her rise through the ranks of the serpent clan, her encounters with various immortals who had sought her favor.

The third page made Xiao Qing's blood run cold.

*Bai Suyin, known as the White Snake, is hereby identified as a vessel of the Primordial Chaos. Her existence was engineered by the Celestial Council to contain the remnants of the Great Dissolution. Her cultivation is not her own—it is borrowed power, meant to stabilize the boundary between realms.*

*She is unaware of her true nature.*

*The Celestial Council has determined that her eventual sealing is necessary to prevent the Chaos from breaking free. The method—to be enacted at the Council's discretion—must involve emotional trauma sufficient to destabilize her core.*

*The agent selected for this task: Fahai of the Jingsi Temple.*

Xiao Qing's hands stopped shaking. They became very still.

She read it again.

And again.

"The Great Dissolution," she whispered. "What is that?"

The Library didn't answer. But the old woman did.

"Before the current celestial order, there was another. Older. More powerful. It destroyed itself in a war that lasted ten thousand years. The remnants of that war—the Chaos—could not be destroyed. Only contained." She paused. "The White Snake was created to be that containment."

"Created." Xiao Qing's voice was flat. "She wasn't born. She was made."

"All things are made, little snake. The question is by whom and for what purpose."

Xiao Qing looked back at the book. There was more. She turned the page.

*Bai Suyin's sister, Xiao Qing, was not part of the original design. Her existence is anomalous. The Celestial Council has debated eliminating her multiple times but has been unable to do so—she is protected by a clause in the original agreement that forbids interference with anomalies.*

*Recommendation: Monitor Xiao Qing closely. If she becomes a threat to the containment protocol, eliminate her regardless of the clause.*

Xiao Qing laughed. It was not a pleasant sound.

"Eliminate me," she said. "They wanted to eliminate me from the beginning."

"The Celestial Council is thorough," the old woman said. "They don't like loose ends."

"I'm not a loose end." Xiao Qing closed the book. "I'm the one who's going to unravel their entire design."

She looked at the old woman. "Where is the Chaos? The remnants of the Great Dissolution. Where are they contained now?"

The old woman's eyes widened. "You can't be serious. If you release that—"

"I'm not going to release it. I'm going to use it."

"Use it? Child, the Chaos is not a tool. It's destruction given form. It doesn't serve—it consumes."

"Then I'll make it serve." Xiao Qing tucked the book into her robe. "My sister was made to be a cage. I'm going to find out what was caged. And then I'm going to break the lock."

The old woman stepped back. For the first time, she looked afraid.

"You don't understand what you're asking."

"Then explain it to me."

"The Chaos is not a thing. It's an absence. A hunger. It was the first force, before order, before balance. It

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Price of Remembering

The golden bowl hummed.

It sat in the center of a room that shouldn't exist—a chamber folded between breaths, tucked behind a waterfall that had dried up three thousand years ago. The bowl was old. Older than the temple that once housed it. Older than the mountain the temple had been built on.

And now it was awake.

A hand reached out and touched its surface. Old skin, thin as paper, spotted with age. The fingers traced the characters etched into the bronze—names of demons bound, spirits sealed, snakes captured.

One name at the top glowed faintly green.

"Not yet," the voice said. "But soon."

The hand withdrew. The room fell silent again.

But the bowl kept humming.

---

Xiao Qing walked through the night.

She didn't stop to rest. Her ribs ached with every step, the cracked bones grinding against each other, but she'd healed worse. Five hundred years of solitary cultivation had taught her to ignore pain. Pain was information. Pain told you what was still alive.

Her left hand stayed closed around Elder Xu's bone.

It was warm. Not the warmth of living flesh, but something else. Residual qi, yes, but also something older. Memory. The bone remembered being part of something whole. It remembered the body it had belonged to, the meridians that had carried power through it, the techniques that had shaped water into weapons and shields.

She could feel echoes of those techniques if she concentrated. Faint impressions, like footprints in wet sand.

But she didn't have time to study them now.

The Library of Forgotten Things didn't wait. It moved through the spaces between realms, drifting like a ship without anchor. If she didn't find the key soon, the library would shift again, and it might be another century before it resurfaced.

And she didn't have a century.

She had days, maybe hours, before the celestial court realized what she was looking for.

---

Dawn found her at the edge of a forest that wasn't on any map.

The trees here were wrong. Their trunks bent at angles that violated geometry, their leaves reflected light that didn't exist in the visible spectrum. The soil beneath her feet hummed with a frequency that made her teeth ache.

This was the border. The place where the mortal realm frayed into something else.

Xiao Qing stopped at the treeline and listened.

The wind carried voices—not words, but the shape of words. Conversation without meaning. The forest was alive with the echoes of things that had been said and forgotten.

"Perfect," she muttered.

She stepped forward.

The world turned inside out.

---

For a moment, she was everywhere and nowhere. Her body stretched across dimensions, her consciousness splintering into a thousand fragments, each one experiencing a different version of the same moment. She felt herself falling and rising and standing still, all at once.

Then she was solid again.

The Library of Forgotten Things rose before her.

It wasn't a building. It was a *presence*—a space that had decided to look like a building so that mortal minds could comprehend it. Walls of stone that breathed. Windows that blinked. A door that watched her approach with patient hunger.

"Welcome," the door said. Its voice was the sound of pages turning. "You are expected."

Xiao Qing stopped. "Expected?"

"Expected." The door's surface rippled. "The library knows when someone carries a question that needs answering. You have carried yours for five hundred years."

She didn't like that. She didn't like that anything knew she was coming.

"I need the original registry," she said. "The one that predates the current celestial administration."

"The registry of truths." The door's hinges creaked—a sound like old laughter. "It has not been opened in three thousand years. Do you know the price?"

"I know I need a key."

"The key is sacrifice." The door swung open, revealing darkness so complete it seemed to drink light. "But not the kind you think. Not blood. Not life. Memory."

Xiao Qing's hand tightened around Elder Xu's bone.

"What kind of memory?"

"Your most precious. The one you hold closest. The one that defines who you are." The darkness pulsed. "Give it up, and you may enter. Refuse, and you will never find what you seek."

She thought of her sister's face. The way Bai Suyin smiled when she thought no one was watching. The warmth of her hand when she'd pulled Xiao Qing from the river, all those centuries ago, when she was just a green snake drowning in her own fear.

She thought of the last time she'd seen her sister—the chains, the pagoda, Fahai's cold eyes.

"No," Xiao Qing said. "I won't give that up."

The door was silent for a long moment.

"Then you misunderstand," it said finally. "The library does not *take* memories. It *binds* them. You will still possess the memory. But you will not be able to access it. Not until you leave. And if you fail to leave..." The darkness shifted. "Then the memory remains here. Forever."

A prison for the mind.

Xiao Qing looked at the bone in her hand. Elder Xu had given his life for her. He'd burned so she could escape. She carried his last fragment with her, a promise made in ash.

But what would she be willing to sacrifice for her sister?

She already knew the answer.

"Which memory?"

"The one that makes you human."

---

She walked into the darkness.

The door closed behind her with a sound like a tomb sealing shut.

And then she was in the library.

Rows upon rows of shelves stretched in every direction, defying perspective. Books of every size and color lined them—some small enough to fit in a palm, others large enough to require ladders. The air smelled of dust and ink and something metallic. Blood, maybe. Or time.

A path opened before her, guiding her deeper.

She followed it.

The books on either side seemed to watch her as she passed. Their spines bore titles in languages she didn't recognize, scripts that shifted and changed when she tried to read them. But she caught fragments: *The Last Breath of Dying Stars. The Weight of Unspoken Words. The Geometry of Regret.*

Every book here held something forgotten.

Every book here was a tomb.

The path ended at a pedestal. On it rested a single scroll, bound with red silk that had faded to pink. Characters were embroidered into the silk—ancient script, predating the current celestial dynasty by millennia.

The original registry.

Xiao Qing's hands trembled as she reached for it.

"Touch it," a voice said, "and you accept the terms."

She turned. A figure stood in the shadows between two shelves—tall, impossibly thin, with skin the color of old paper. Its eyes were empty sockets filled with crawling text.

"The memory," the figure said. "Give it."

Xiao Qing closed her eyes.

She thought of the memory she was about to lose. The first time she'd taken human form. The rain on her skin. The terror of standing on two legs. And her sister's voice, soft and patient: *"You're beautiful, little one. Don't be afraid."*

That memory had carried her through five centuries of loneliness.

"Take it," she whispered.

Something reached into her mind. Cold. Precise. It found the memory, wrapped around it, and pulled.

She gasped. Pain exploded behind her eyes—not physical pain, but something worse. The pain of loss. The pain of a hole where something precious used to be.

And then it was gone.

She couldn't remember what she'd lost. She only knew that something was missing. Something important.

The figure bowed and vanished into the shadows.

The scroll was in her hands.

---

She opened it.

The registry was written on material that wasn't paper—something thinner and stronger, like dried serpent skin. The characters glowed faintly as she read them, names and dates and deeds stretching back to the beginning of the celestial order.

She found her sister's name near the middle.

*Bai Suyin. White Snake. Cultivator. Incarnation of the Serpent Queen's bloodline. Status: Sealed under Leifeng Pagoda by decree of the Celestial Court. Reason: Violation of the boundary between mortal and celestial realms.*

There was more. Details of the trial. Testimonies. A list of charges.

But at the bottom, written in a different hand—hastier, as if added as an afterthought—was a single line that made her blood freeze:

*Note: The sealing is incomplete. The White Snake's essence was divided. One third remains unaccounted for. Search ongoing.*

Her sister's soul.

Part of it was still out there.

Xiao Qing read the line again. And again. Her hands shook so hard the scroll rattled.

If she could find that missing piece, she could restore her sister. Not fully—not without breaking the seal entirely—but enough. Enough to speak to her. Enough to touch her. Enough to *save* her.

She looked for more information. The registry listed locations, investigation reports, the names of celestial agents assigned to the search. But the entries grew sparse toward the end, then stopped entirely.

The search had been abandoned.

Someone had decided that one third of a White Snake's soul wasn't worth finding.

Rage burned through her—cold and clean and sharp as a blade.

She rolled the scroll carefully, tucking it into her sleeve. Then she turned and walked back through the library, past the watching books, past the shifting shadows.

The door opened before she reached it.

"You found what you needed," the door said. It wasn't a question.

"I found a start." Xiao Qing stepped into the light. The forest swayed around her, the wrong trees bending in the wrong directions. "Now I need to find the rest."

"The memory will return to you once you leave this space."

It did. The moment her feet touched mortal soil, the memory flooded back—her sister's voice, the rain, the terror and joy of her first human step. She stumbled, overwhelmed, and caught herself against a tree.

The bark was warm. Alive.

She pressed her forehead against it and breathed.

*One third of her sister's soul. Lost. Forgotten. Hidden somewhere in the three realms.*

She would find it.

She would find it if she had to tear the celestial court apart stone by stone.

---

The golden bowl stopped humming.

The old hand withdrew, trembling.

"She found the registry," the voice whispered. "She knows."

Silence.

"Prepare the vessel. We have very little time."

In the distance, thunder rolled across a cloudless sky.

And in a pagoda that had stood for five hundred years, a White Snake stirred in her sleep for the first time in centuries.

title: The Price of Memory
content:
The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, following the traces of old qi that only someone who had cultivated in complete isolation for five centuries could sense. The trail led her through forests that shifted when she wasn't looking, across rivers that flowed backward, and finally to a cliff that dropped into infinite mist.

She stood at the edge, the jade pendant warm against her chest. Elder Xu's bone fragment was still tucked in her sleeve, a constant reminder of the price others had paid for her quest.

"There's nothing here," she said aloud.

The mist did not answer.

She closed her eyes. Elder Xu had once told her that the most powerful things in the three realms were not hidden by barriers or guarded by beasts. They were hidden by *indifference*. A library that existed between realms would not respond to force. It would respond to *need*.

She thought of her sister. The way Bai Suyin had smiled when teaching her to transform. The way she'd held Xiao Qing's hand during the thunder tribulation. The way she'd looked at Fahai with something like pity in her final moments.

*I don't need the truth for myself,* she thought. *I need it for her.*

The mist parted.

A stone path materialized beneath her feet, leading downward into the cliff face. Xiao Qing did not hesitate. She stepped forward, and the world twisted around her.

---

The Library was not a building. It was a *space*—a vast, endless cavern where shelves stretched in every direction, defying gravity and logic. Books floated in clusters like constellations. Scrolls hung suspended in midair, their contents visible only to those who knew how to look.

And in the center of it all sat a figure.

She was old. Not in the way of mortals, with wrinkles and gray hair, but in the way of ancient things—her skin had the texture of parchment, her eyes the depth of inkwells. She wore robes of shifting gray, and her fingers were long and bony, like the roots of an old tree.

"A visitor," the woman said. Her voice was dry, like pages turning. "It has been... a very long time."

Xiao Qing bowed. "I seek the original registry of the celestial court. Specifically, the records from the White Snake's trial."

The woman's eyes flickered with something like amusement. "Bold. Most who come here ask for cultivation manuals or forbidden techniques. You ask for *bureaucracy*."

"The truth is often found in paperwork."

"A girl after my own heart." The woman rose, her movements fluid despite her ancient appearance. "I am the Keeper. This is my domain. And you cannot enter without a key."

"The key requires a sacrifice."

"Correct." The Keeper circled Xiao Qing, studying her. "You've already made one sacrifice, I see. A piece of bone from an old cultivator. A fragment of memory." She stopped, tilting her head. "But that was for *finding* me. Not for entering."

"What do you want?"

The Keeper smiled, and it was not a pleasant expression. "I want what you carry in your chest, little snake. Not your life. Something smaller. A memory. The most painful one you have."

Xiao Qing's blood ran cold. "Why?"

"Because pain is the currency of truth. The Library feeds on it. The older the pain, the deeper the record it can access." The Keeper leaned closer. "You have a memory from five hundred years ago. The moment you realized you couldn't save her. I can see it in your aura—a crack that never healed."

She was right. Xiao Qing could still feel it—the moment she'd arrived at Leifeng Pagoda, only to see the last stone fall into place. The moment she'd heard her sister's voice through the stone, telling her to *run*.

"Take it," Xiao Qing said. Her voice was steady, but her hands were shaking.

"Are you certain? Once given, it cannot be returned. You will remember the event, but the *pain* will be gone. The lesson will fade. You may find yourself making the same mistakes again."

"I said take it."

The Keeper reached out and pressed her palm against Xiao Qing's forehead.

The world went white.

---

She was standing at the foot of Leifeng Pagoda.

The sky was gray, heavy with rain that refused to fall. The air smelled of incense and blood. Monks chanted in a language she couldn't understand, their voices rising and falling like waves.

And there, at the top of the pagoda, was Bai Suyin.

She was beautiful even in chains. Her white robes were torn, her hair loose, her face calm. She looked down at Xiao Qing with an expression of infinite sadness.

"Little sister," Bai Suyin said. Her voice carried despite the chanting. "You shouldn't have come."

"I'm not leaving you."

"You must. Fahai has already won. This was always going to be the ending."

Xiao Qing tried to fly upward, but something held her back. Invisible chains, woven from the same suppression runes that held her sister. She struggled, screamed, clawed at the air—

"Remember what I taught you," Bai Suyin said. "When the world tries to trap you, become water. Flow around it. And when the time is right—"

"*Flood it*," Xiao Qing whispered.

Bai Suyin smiled. "Yes."

The last stone fell into place. The pagoda sealed. The chanting stopped. And Xiao Qing was left alone, her sister's voice echoing in her head.

*Run.*

She ran.

And she never stopped running.

---

The memory faded.

Xiao Qing opened her eyes. She was on her knees in the Library, gasping. The Keeper stood over her, holding something that glowed like a pearl—the extracted memory.

"Fascinating," the Keeper murmured. "Your sister knew she was going to lose. And she still chose to fight." She looked at Xiao Qing with new respect. "That kind of love is rare."

"Give me the registry."

The Keeper gestured, and a scroll floated down from the upper shelves. It was old—older than anything Xiao Qing had ever seen. The parchment was yellowed, the edges frayed. It unrolled in the air, revealing columns of names and judgments.

"Bai Suyin," the Keeper said. "Let me see."

She scanned the scroll, her bony finger tracing the characters. Then she stopped.

"Oh."

"What?" Xiao Qing demanded. "What does it say?"

The Keeper looked up, her expression unreadable. "According to this registry, your sister was never supposed to be sealed. The original judgment was *execution*. Fahai changed it."

"That's not surprising. He was always merciful."

"There's more." The Keeper's voice dropped. "The original accuser in the trial—the one who brought the initial charges against Bai Suyin—was not the celestial court. It was a mortal."

Xiao Qing's blood went cold. "A mortal? Impossible. Mortals don't have standing in celestial court."

"They do if they're a cultivator who has transcended mortality but refused ascension. Someone who chose to remain in the mortal realm." The Keeper squinted at the text. "The name is here. It's... faded. Deliberately scratched out. But I can read the remnants."

"Tell me."

"Xu Xian."

The world stopped.

Xiao Qing stared at the Keeper, her mind refusing to process the name. "That's not possible. Xu Xian loved her. He married her. He—"

"He was also terrified of her." The Keeper rolled the scroll closed. "Fear and love are not mutually exclusive, little snake. And fear often wins."

"But he *died*. He died of old age, decades before Fahai sealed her. How could he have—"

"He didn't die." The Keeper's voice was flat. "The records show that Xu Xian faked his death and continued cultivating in secret. He reached a level of power that rivaled the celestial court. And he used that power to destroy your sister."

Xiao Qing's hands clenched into fists. "Where is he now?"

"I don't know. The records end fifty years ago. But there is one thing I can tell you." The Keeper leaned forward. "The suppression runes on Leifeng Pagoda? The ones that hold your sister? They were designed by Xu Xian. He knew her weaknesses better than anyone."

The jade pendant burned against Xiao Qing's chest. The bone fragment in her sleeve seemed to pulse with anger.

Five hundred years.

Five hundred years of believing her sister's lover had been a victim. Five hundred years of blaming Fahai, blaming the celestial court, blaming everyone except the one person who had the most to gain.

Xu Xian.

"Where do I find him?" Xiao Qing's voice was barely a whisper.

"I told you. The records end fifty years ago. He vanished." The Keeper paused. "But there is a rumor. A temple in the eastern mountains. One that appears only during the new moon. They say an immortal lives there, studying the nature of *transformation*."

"Transformation?"

"Turning mortals into monsters. Turning love into hate." The Keeper's eyes glittered. "If I were you, I'd start there."

Xiao Qing stood. Her legs were unsteady, but her resolve was iron. She bowed once to the Keeper.

"I owe you a debt."

"You paid it with your pain." The Keeper held up the glowing pearl. "I'll keep this safe. Perhaps, when this is over, you'll want it back."

"I won't."

She turned and walked toward the exit. The Library's space shifted around her, guiding her back to the cliff's edge. When she stepped out, the mist closed behind her, and the path vanished.

The stars were out. The wind was cold. And somewhere in the eastern mountains, a temple was waiting.

Xiao Qing began to walk.

---

She traveled through the night, her mind racing. Xu Xian. The man her sister had loved. The man who had held her hand and watched her smile and whispered sweet words into her ear.

And then betrayed her.

Why? What could possibly drive someone to destroy the person they loved most?

She didn't have an answer. But she would find one.

By dawn, she reached the foothills of the eastern mountains. The terrain was treacherous—rockslides, hidden ravines, and the lingering qi of old battles. But she pressed on, following the Keeper's instructions.

The new moon was three days away.

She found a cave to shelter in as the sun rose. Her body was exhausted, her cultivation drained. She needed rest.

But sleep did not come easily.

Every time she closed her eyes, she saw Xu Xian's face. Not as he was—an old man, a corpse—but as he had been. Young. Handsome. Kind.

*Liar,* she thought. *You were always a liar.*

She touched the bone fragment in her sleeve. Elder Xu's last words echoed in her mind.

*Remember who you are.*

"I remember," she whispered. "I'm the one who's going to end this."

---

She woke to the sound of footsteps.

Her eyes snapped open. The cave was dark, the fire she'd built reduced to embers. But she could sense someone outside. Someone powerful.

She rose silently, her claws extending.

"Xiao Qing," a voice said. Familiar. Too familiar.

She stepped out of the cave.

The man standing there was young. Handsome. Dressed in white robes that seemed to glow in the moonlight.

Xu Xian.

Except it couldn't be.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Price of a Memory

The Library of Forgotten Things did not welcome visitors.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, then east, then in directions that had no names. The path to the library wasn't a path at all—it was a series of thresholds that existed only when the stars aligned in patterns that hadn't occurred in three thousand years.

She couldn't wait three thousand years.

So she found another way.

The village appeared at twilight, nestled between two hills that shouldn't have been there. Xiao Qing had passed this exact spot twice before, and both times there had been nothing but empty grassland. But now, smoke rose from chimneys. Children played in the dusty streets. An old woman sat on a porch, shelling peas.

Xiao Qing stopped at the village edge.

The old woman looked up. Her eyes were milky white, blind, but she stared directly at Xiao Qing with the unsettling precision of someone who saw more than the physical world.

"You're early," the old woman said.

"I didn't know there was a schedule."

"There isn't." The old woman returned to her peas. "But you're early anyway. The library doesn't open for another seventy-two years."

"I can't wait that long."

"Most can't." The old woman's fingers moved with practiced ease, shells falling into a basket. "They die first. Or forget what they were looking for. Or find it somewhere else and realize they didn't need the library at all."

Xiao Qing stepped into the village. The children didn't look at her. The adults didn't stop their work. But she felt their attention like pressure on her skin—hundreds of eyes watching from behind windows, from beneath hats, from the corners of mouths that didn't smile.

She stopped in front of the old woman's porch.

"I need the original registry," Xiao Qing said. "The one that predates the celestial purge. The one that holds the truth about my sister."

The old woman's hands stilled. For a long moment, she didn't move. Then she looked up again, and this time her milky eyes glowed with faint silver light.

"You want to read what the heavens burned."

"Yes."

"The registry is protected by three seals. The first is knowledge—you must prove you understand what you're asking for. The second is sacrifice—you must give something you cannot reclaim. The third is truth—you must face what you find without turning away."

Xiao Qing's hand moved to her sleeve, where Elder Xu's bone rested against her wrist. "I understand."

"No." The old woman's voice sharpened. "You understand what I said. You don't understand what it means. Let me make it clearer." She set down her peas. "The first seal will test your mind. The second will break your heart. The third will show you that everything you've believed for five hundred years is wrong."

The village went silent. The children stopped playing. The adults stopped working. Every face turned toward Xiao Qing, and every face wore the same expression—cold, expectant, hungry.

Xiao Qing felt her cultivation stir, defensive instincts rising. She suppressed them.

"Show me the first seal," she said.

The old woman smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"Child," she said, "you've already passed it."

The world twisted.

Xiao Qing stood alone in darkness. No village. No old woman. No sky. Just endless black, and in the distance, a single point of light.

She walked toward it.

The light grew as she approached, resolving into a room. A circular room with walls covered in writing—characters so old they predated the celestial script, predated the mortal languages, predated anything Xiao Qing had ever learned.

At the center of the room stood a pedestal. On the pedestal rested a scroll, bound with green silk.

Xiao Qing's heart stopped.

The silk was the exact shade of her sister's favorite robe.

She reached for the scroll, and the writing on the walls began to glow. Not light—heat. The characters burned, and the room grew hot, and the air turned thick with smoke that smelled of incense and blood.

"The second seal," said a voice that came from everywhere and nowhere. "Give what you cannot reclaim."

Xiao Qing's hand hovered over the scroll. "What do you want?"

"Your memory."

"Which one?"

"The one that matters most."

She closed her eyes. Her mind raced through five centuries of memories—her sister's laughter, the training sessions, the night they fled the celestial palace, the moment she watched Bai Suyin sink beneath the golden chains of Leifeng Pagoda.

Which mattered most?

The answer came instantly, and it broke her heart.

She opened her eyes. "Take it."

The heat vanished. The writing on the walls went dark. And in the space where a memory had been, there was nothing. A hollow. A wound that would never heal.

She couldn't remember her sister's face.

Xiao Qing stood frozen, her hand still reaching for the scroll. She knew she had a sister. She knew she loved her. She knew she was doing this to save her. But when she tried to picture Bai Suyin's features—the curve of her smile, the color of her eyes, the way she tilted her head when she was amused—there was only blankness.

Tears streamed down Xiao Qing's face. She didn't know why she was crying. She only knew the grief was unbearable.

"The scroll," she whispered, her voice cracking.

She unrolled it.

The characters were written in silver ink that seemed to move, shifting and reforming as she read. The original registry. The truth before the purge. The record of every being who had ever been struck from celestial history.

She found her sister's name.

And then she found the truth.

Xiao Qing read it once, then again, then a third time because she couldn't believe what her eyes were telling her.

Bai Suyin had not been a rogue cultivator who stole celestial secrets.

Bai Suyin had been the one who created them.

The registry detailed a war that had been erased from history—a war between the original celestial court and something older, something that had existed before the heavens were formed. The current gods had not won that war. They had survived it, barely, by making a deal.

The deal was sealed with blood.

Her sister's blood.

Bai Suyin had been the architect of the celestial court's greatest achievements. She had designed the systems of cultivation that mortals now used. She had written the laws that governed qi. She had built the foundations of the three realms.

And when the old gods grew afraid of her power, they had erased her.

Not killed her—they couldn't kill her. They had sealed her beneath Leifeng Pagoda, buried her so deep that even her name became forbidden. They had rewritten history to make her a thief, a criminal, a serpent who had overreached.

Fahai had not been a righteous monk.

He had been an executioner, following orders from the highest seats in heaven.

Xiao Qing's hands trembled. The scroll shook in her grip.

"Third seal," she said, her voice barely audible. "Face the truth without turning away."

She had passed.

But the truth was destroying her.

She stood in the circular room, surrounded by walls of dead writing, holding proof that everything was a lie. Her sister had not been a criminal. Her sister had been a god. And the celestial court had buried her alive rather than share power.

The jade pendant around Xiao Qing's neck grew warm.

She looked down at it, and for the first time, she noticed something she'd never seen before—a crack running through the center, thin as a hair, barely visible. The pendant was damaged.

No. Not damaged.

Activated.

"The third seal," the voice said again, softer now, almost gentle. "You have faced the truth. But can you bear what comes next?"

The scroll in Xiao Qing's hands began to burn. Not with fire—with light. Silver light that poured from the characters, flooding the room, filling her vision until she could see nothing else.

When the light faded, she was no longer in the library.

She was standing in a temple that didn't exist on any map.

Golden bowls lined the walls, each one humming with suppressed power. And at the center of the room, suspended in chains of pure light, was a figure she recognized despite never seeing him before.

A man. Old, withered, barely alive.

But his eyes were open, and they were the same shade of green as her own.

"Xiao Qing," he whispered. "You found me faster than I expected."

She didn't know his voice. She didn't know his face. But some instinct, some primal recognition, made her knees buckle.

"Who are you?"

The old man smiled, and his teeth were stained with blood. "I'm the one who taught your sister everything she knew. I'm the one who built the library. I'm the one who's been waiting for five hundred years for someone to ask the right questions." His chains tightened, and he winced. "And I'm the one who's about to die because the celestial court finally found me."

The golden bowls began to hum louder.

"Listen carefully," the old man said, his voice urgent now. "Your sister isn't just sealed. She's being drained. Every day, every hour, the pagoda siphons her power and sends it to the heavens. The gods have been feeding on her for five centuries. That's how they've stayed strong while the old powers faded."

Xiao Qing's blood turned to ice.

"Leifeng Pagoda isn't a prison," the old man continued. "It's a pipeline. And if you break it open without understanding what's connected to it, you won't free your sister. You'll kill every god in heaven. And then the balance of the three realms will collapse, and everything will burn."

The first golden bowl shattered.

The old man screamed.

"Go," he gasped. "Find the jade master. He's the only one who knows how to sever the connection without destroying everything. He's in the eastern marshes, hiding under a name he stole from a dead man. Find him before they find you."

Xiao Qing grabbed his hand. "I'm not leaving you."

"You have to." His grip was surprisingly strong. "Because I'm not the only prisoner they're keeping. And the others—" His eyes rolled back, white replacing green. "The others are waking up."

The second bowl shattered.

The temple began to collapse.

Xiao Qing ran.

She burst through the doors into a night sky that was wrong—the stars were moving, rearranging themselves into patterns she'd never seen. The ground beneath her feet was cracking, revealing light that came from somewhere deep, somewhere ancient, somewhere that had been buried for longer than the celestial court had existed.

Behind her, the old man's screams stopped.

In front of her, the eastern marshes stretched into darkness.

And from every direction, she heard the sound of golden bowls breaking, one after another, spreading across the three realms like a plague of awakening.

She ran faster.

The jade pendant burned against her chest, and somewhere in the back of her mind, a voice that sounded like her sister whispered:

*They're coming. All of them. And they're not happy about being forgotten.*

The ground opened beneath her feet, and Xiao Qing fell.

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Price of Remembering

The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent seven days walking south, following threads of old qi that whispered through the mountain passes like half-remembered dreams.
