The terrain shifted each night—rocks became rivers, rivers became cliffs, cliffs became empty air that nearly swallowed her whole. The pocket realm was testing her. Proving whether she deserved to find it.

She proved herself by not dying.

On the eighth day, she arrived at a place that shouldn't exist—a valley between two peaks that she'd passed three times already, only noticing it when she stopped trying to notice anything at all. The moment her mind went quiet, the valley appeared. A stone archway covered in vines. A door with no handle.

And a keeper who had been waiting for five hundred years.

"You're early," the woman said.

She sat on a rock beside the archway, dressed in robes so faded they might have been any color once. Her face was ageless—neither young nor old, but worn smooth by time like river stones. She held a tea cup that had no tea in it.

Xiao Qing stopped ten feet away. "I don't believe we've met."

"No." The woman smiled. "But I know your sister."

The words hit like a physical blow. Xiao Qing's hand moved to her sleeve, where Elder Xu's bone rested against her wrist. "Who are you?"

"A librarian." The woman set down the empty cup. "I've kept the records here for longer than your species has walked upright. I watched your sister's story unfold the first time. I've been watching it unfold again, in fragments, through the ripples you've been making."

"I haven't made ripples."

"You killed six celestial guards in Yunding. You freed Elder Xu from his prison—though he was already dead by then, wasn't he?" The librarian's eyes held no judgment. "And you stole from the Heavenly Court's own archives. Copies of documents you shouldn't have been able to reach."

Xiao Qing's blood ran cold. "How do you know that?"

"I told you. I'm a librarian. I know everything that gets written down." The woman stood, brushing dust from her robes. "And I know what you're looking for. The original registry. The one that predates Fahai's administration. The one that tells the truth about why Bai Suyin was really sealed."

"Can you give it to me?"

"No."

The answer was flat. Final.

Xiao Qing's hands curled into fists. "Then why are you here?"

"To warn you." The librarian walked toward her, slow and deliberate. "The original registry exists. I won't deny that. I've held it in my hands, read every page. But it's not in my library anymore. It was moved seven hundred years ago, when the celestial court realized that certain truths were dangerous."

"Moved where?"

"To the only place in the three realms that can keep a secret from everyone—including the gods." The librarian stopped inches from Xiao Qing. She was shorter than expected, but her presence pressed against Xiao Qing's skin like heat. "The Serpent's Hollow."

Xiao Qing's breath caught.

The Serpent's Hollow. Every snake demon knew that name. It was a legend whispered in the dark places where demons gathered—a prison within a prison, a void where the celestial court sent beings they wanted to forget. Not kill. Forget. As if erasing someone's existence from memory was kinder than death.

"It's not a prison," the librarian said, reading her expression. "Not anymore. It was sealed after the great purge, when the old snake clans were hunted to extinction. The celestial court couldn't destroy what was inside, so they locked it away and erased its location from every map."

"Then how do I find it?"

"You don't find it. You earn it." The librarian reached into her sleeve and pulled out a jade token—identical to the one Xiao Qing had taken from Elder Xu's cell. But this one pulsed with a different energy. Cold. Hungry. "The Serpent's Hollow only opens to one who carries the blood of the old serpent kings. Not the common snakes that crawl in the grass. The ones who ruled before the celestial court existed."

Xiao Qing stared at the token. "I don't have that blood."

"No. But you carry the bone of someone who did."

Elder Xu.

Xiao Qing's hand went to her sleeve again, where the unburned bone rested. She'd thought it was just residual qi, old cultivation clinging to death. But the librarian was saying it was more. It was lineage.

"Elder Xu was one of the last," the librarian said softly. "He hid among the white snakes for centuries, but he was never one of them. He was old blood. The kind that predates the division of the three realms."

"Then why didn't he use it himself?"

"Because he was protecting it." The librarian's eyes darkened. "He was waiting for someone worthy to carry it. Someone who would use it not for revenge, but for truth." She paused. "I think you're that someone, Xiao Qing. But I also think you're not ready."

Xiao Qing's jaw tightened. "I've been ready for five hundred years."

"You've been angry for five hundred years. That's different." The librarian held out the jade token. "The Serpent's Hollow will test you. It will show you things you don't want to see. It will ask you to sacrifice things you don't want to give. If you fail—and most do—you'll be trapped there forever. Forgotten. Just like everyone else they sent there."

"Then I won't fail."

"You sound certain."

"I sound like someone who has nothing left to lose."

The librarian studied her for a long moment. Then she smiled—a sad, knowing smile that made Xiao Qing feel like a child again. "That's exactly what concerns me."

She pressed the jade token into Xiao Qing's palm. It was cold, almost painfully so, and it pulsed against her skin like a second heartbeat.

"Follow the token's pull. It will lead you to the Hollow's entrance. But remember—" The librarian's grip tightened. "The Hollow doesn't just hold records. It holds the ghosts of everyone who was sacrificed to create those records. They will try to stop you. They will try to convince you that the truth isn't worth knowing."

"What if they're right?"

The librarian's smile faded. "Then you'll become one of them."

She stepped back, and the valley began to dissolve. The archway faded. The vines curled into nothing. The librarian herself became transparent, her voice echoing as she disappeared.

"Be careful, little snake. The past is a hungry thing. It will consume you if you let it."

And then she was gone.

Xiao Qing stood alone in an empty field, the jade token cold in her hand, Elder Xu's bone heavy in her sleeve.

She followed the pull.

---

The token led her east, toward the coast, toward places she'd never been. The landscape changed as she traveled—mountain gave way to forest, forest gave way to marsh, marsh gave way to a shoreline that shouldn't have existed. The maps said the eastern sea began here, but the token pulled her forward onto water that held her weight like solid ground.

She walked across the ocean for three days.

On the third night, the token stopped pulling.

She was standing in the middle of the sea, no land in sight, the stars wheeling overhead. The water beneath her feet was dark, so dark she couldn't see her own reflection. The token pulsed once, twice, three times—and then went cold.

A crack opened in the water.

Not a whirlpool. Not a hole. A crack, like glass breaking, revealing darkness beneath. And from that darkness came a voice—old, vast, laughing.

"Finally. Someone who remembers we existed."

Xiao Qing looked down.

The darkness looked back.

And then the water swallowed her whole.

---

She fell for what felt like hours.

The darkness was absolute, pressing against her from all sides, filling her lungs even though she wasn't breathing. She should have been panicking. Instead, she felt calm. The same calm she'd felt when she killed the celestial guards. The same calm she'd felt when Elder Xu burned.

The calm of someone who had already decided she wasn't coming back.

She landed on something soft. Damp. Living.

She opened her eyes.

She was in a cavern so vast she couldn't see the walls. The ceiling was made of bones—snake bones, thousands of them, woven together like a canopy. The floor beneath her was covered in dust and scales and the remnants of ancient rituals. And in the center of the cavern, rising like a mountain from the earth, was a structure that made her heart stop.

A pagoda.

Leifeng Pagoda.

But not the one she remembered. This one was older, darker, covered in runes she'd never seen. It pulsed with a light that wasn't light at all—a cold, hungry energy that reached for her like fingers.

"No," she whispered.

But the token in her hand pulsed in response, and the voice from the darkness spoke again—closer now, right beside her ear.

"Welcome home, little serpent. Your sister has been waiting."

Xiao Qing turned.

The ghost standing behind her wore her sister's face.

But the eyes—the eyes were wrong. Too old. Too knowing. Too hungry.

"I'm not your sister," the ghost said, smiling with Bai Suyin's mouth. "But I've worn her skin before. I'll wear yours, too, if you let me."

Xiao Qing's hand shot out, grabbing the ghost by the throat.

The ghost laughed.

"Good reflexes. But you'll need more than that."

It dissolved in her grip, reforming ten feet away, still wearing her sister's face.

"The original registry is inside the pagoda," it said. "Three floors down. Guarded by the ghosts of everyone who tried to read it before you." It tilted its head, Bai Suyin's hair falling across Bai Suyin's face. "Do you want to know how many have tried?"

Xiao Qing didn't answer.

"Seventeen," the ghost said. "Seventeen snake demons, all carrying old blood, all certain they were ready for the truth." Its smile widened. "Seventeen failures."

"Then I'll be the first success."

The ghost laughed again—a sound that echoed through the bone cavern, shaking dust from the ceiling.

"Brave words. Let's see if you still believe them when you're trapped here forever, screaming into the dark, forgotten by everyone who ever loved you."

Xiao Qing walked toward the pagoda.

The ghost followed, still wearing her sister's face, still laughing.

And somewhere inside the pagoda, three floors down, a book with Xiao Qing's name on it began to write itself.

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Price of Forgotten Things

The Library of Forgotten Things didn't want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, then east, then in circles that made no logical sense. The mountains shifted around her like living things, peaks rearranging themselves when she wasn't looking. Rivers changed course. The stars above her spun in patterns she'd never seen in five centuries of watching the sky.

She was close. She could feel it.

The jade pendant pulsed against her chest, warm and urgent. Elder Xu's bone fragment rested in her sleeve, its residual qi humming like a trapped bee. She'd wrapped it in silk to keep it from attracting attention, but the thing had a will of its own. It wanted to go somewhere.

She let it guide her.

On the fourth day, she found herself standing at the edge of a cliff that shouldn't exist. The map she'd memorized showed flat plains here. The terrain she'd crossed suggested a valley. But here was a cliff, sheer and black, dropping into a fog so thick it looked solid.

The bone fragment in her sleeve vibrated violently.

"This is it," she said.

No response. The wind didn't blow. The birds didn't sing. The world held its breath.

Xiao Qing stepped off the cliff.

The fall lasted three heartbeats. Then the fog caught her—not breaking her fall, but *catching* her, cradling her like a mother's arms. She floated downward, suspended in whiteness that smelled of old paper and dried flowers.

When her feet touched ground, she was standing in a garden.

The Library of Forgotten Things wasn't a building. It was a grove of ancient trees, their trunks carved with characters so old they predated the current celestial script. Between the trees hung scrolls, thousands of them, suspended from branches by silk cords that had never frayed. Lanterns floated overhead, their flames blue and cold.

A woman sat in the center of the grove, writing on a scroll that stretched across her lap and pooled on the ground like a river of paper.

She was ancient. Not in appearance—her face was smooth, ageless, beautiful in the way that mountains are beautiful. But her eyes held depths that spoke of time beyond counting. She wore robes of grey that shifted between shadows, and her hair fell in a single braid that touched the ground and kept going, coiled in a pile beside her.

"You're early," she said without looking up.

Xiao Qing bowed. "I didn't know there was a schedule."

"There isn't." The woman dipped her brush in ink that seemed to absorb light. "But you're early anyway. The other one isn't due for another three hundred years."

"Other one?"

The woman finally looked up. Her eyes were silver, like coins worn smooth by countless hands. She studied Xiao Qing for a long moment, then smiled. It wasn't a warm smile.

"You carry death in your sleeve," she said. "And memory in your chest. And rage in your bones. You're the green snake. I've been expecting you since before you were born."

Xiao Qing's hand moved instinctively to her jade pendant. "Then you know why I'm here."

"I know what you think you need." The woman set down her brush. "The original registry. The one that predates the current administration. You want to find the truth about your sister."

"Yes."

"The truth is not what you think it is."

"Everyone keeps telling me that." Xiao Qing's voice sharpened. "I don't care. I'll find it myself."

The woman laughed, a sound like wind through dead leaves. "Bold. Stupid. But bold." She rose, and her scroll rolled up behind her without being touched. "You're right that the original registry exists. It's here, in this library. But nothing in this place is free. Everything has a price."

"I have currency." Xiao Qing reached into her sleeve.

"No." The woman's voice turned cold. "Not gold. Not jade. Not cultivation resources. The Library of Forgotten Things deals in only one currency."

She stepped closer, and Xiao Qing felt the temperature drop.

"Memories," the woman said. "You want a memory from the library. You must leave a memory of equal value."

Xiao Qing's blood ran cold. "Which memory?"

"That's not how it works." The woman circled her, robes brushing the ground. "The library chooses. I'm just the librarian. I can tell you the price, but I can't negotiate it."

"Then tell me."

The woman stopped in front of her. Those silver eyes met Xiao Qing's, and for a moment, Xiao Qing felt like she was falling again, falling through fog, falling through time.

"Your sister's face," the woman said. "The library wants the memory of your sister's face."

The world stopped.

"No," Xiao Qing whispered.

"Take a moment to consider." The woman's voice was gentle now, almost kind. "The memory of her face. That's the price. You'll remember that you had a sister. You'll remember her name, her voice, her love. But you won't be able to picture her. Her face will be a blank space in your mind. A hole where she used to be."

"Absolutely not."

"Then you'll never see the registry."

"I'll find another way."

"There is no other way." The woman's silver eyes held no mercy. "The original registry exists in only one place. This is that place. I am the only one who can retrieve it. And the price is non-negotiable."

Xiao Qing's hands were shaking. She clenched them into fists, felt her nails bite into her palms.

"Five hundred years," she said, her voice cracking. "Five hundred years I've held her face in my mind. Every detail. The curve of her smile. The way her eyes crinkled when she laughed. The way she looked at me like I was worth something, even when I was nothing. You're asking me to give that up."

"I'm not asking," the woman said. "I'm telling you the price. Whether you pay it is your choice."

Xiao Qing thought of Elder Xu, burning in those chains. Thought of the empty cell, the suppression runes, the celestial court that had rewritten her sister's history. Thought of the golden bowl in the temple that no longer existed.

She thought of her sister's face.

"I'll do it," she said.

The woman nodded, unsurprised. "Sit."

Xiao Qing sat cross-legged on the ground. The woman sat across from her, produced a silver needle from her sleeve. It was long, thin, glinting with cold light.

"This will hurt," she said.

"Everything hurts."

The needle touched Xiao Qing's temple.

The pain was indescribable. It wasn't physical—it was deeper, a tearing of something that had no name. Xiao Qing felt her mind being opened, felt hands sifting through her memories like someone rifling through papers. She tried to hold onto her sister's face, tried to grip it with every fiber of her being.

The needle pulled.

And her sister's face was gone.

Xiao Qing gasped, falling forward, catching herself on her hands. Her vision swam. She blinked, trying to remember—

She had a sister.

She knew that. She loved her sister. Her sister's name was Bai Suyin, and she was sealed under Leifeng Pagoda, and Xiao Qing had spent five centuries trying to free her.

But when she tried to picture her sister's face—

Nothing.

A blank. A void. A shape that should have been filled but wasn't.

Tears streamed down her face. She didn't know why she was crying. She just knew that something precious had been taken from her, and she would never get it back.

"The registry," she said, her voice raw.

The woman was already walking toward the largest tree in the grove. Its trunk was wider than a house, its bark covered in characters that glowed faintly. She placed her hand on the trunk, and a section of bark slid aside, revealing a hollow space.

Inside was a single scroll, bound with red silk.

"This is it," the woman said. "The original registry of demon cultivation. Compiled before the celestial court's reorganization. Before the rules were rewritten." She held it out. "Read it quickly. You have until the lanterns go dark."

Xiao Qing took the scroll with trembling hands. She unwound the silk, unrolled the paper.

The characters were ancient, written in vermillion ink that hadn't faded in millennia. She scanned them quickly, her heart pounding.

Names. Thousands of names. Demons who had cultivated in the early days, before the celestial court had established its monopoly on power. The registry listed their species, their cultivation methods, their achievements.

And then she found it.

*Bai Suyin. White serpent. Celestial-grade cultivation. Method: Jade Serpent Scripture (authenticated). Status: Ascended.*

Xiao Qing stared at the word. *Ascended.*

Not "sealed." Not "imprisoned." Not "executed for crimes against heaven."

*Ascended.*

She read the entry again, then again, her eyes tracing every character.

*Bai Suyin. White serpent. Celestial-grade cultivation. Method: Jade Serpent Scripture (authenticated). Status: Ascended.*

*Note: Subject achieved ascension through orthodox cultivation. No violations of celestial law recorded. Subject voluntarily entered sealing under Leifeng Pagoda per request of celestial court, terms to be negotiated.*

"Voluntarily," Xiao Qing breathed. "She volunteered."

"That's what it says," the woman said from behind her.

"But Fahai—the celestial court—they said she was a criminal. They said she—"

"The celestial court lies." The woman's voice was flat. "You know this. You've always known this."

Xiao Qing's hands were shaking so hard the scroll rattled. "Why would she volunteer? Why would she let them seal her?"

"Read the rest."

There was more. Smaller characters at the bottom of the entry, almost invisible against the aged paper.

*Terms of agreement: Bai Suyin agrees to enter sealing for period of one thousand years. In exchange, celestial court agrees to: (1) Grant protection to all serpent-kind for duration of sealing. (2) Grant safe passage and cultivation resources to subject's younger sister, Xiao Qing. (3) Maintain secrecy of this agreement until subject's release.*

*Witnessed and sealed by: The Celestial Emperor (previous administration).*

Xiao Qing's legs gave out. She sank to her knees, the scroll clutched to her chest.

Her sister had made a deal. A deal to protect her. A deal to protect all serpent-kind. And the celestial court had broken it. They'd rewritten history, vilified Bai Suyin, hunted serpent-kind to near extinction. They'd taken Xiao Qing's sister and made her a monster in the eyes of the three realms.

And Xiao Qing had spent five centuries thinking she was fighting to free an innocent prisoner.

She was.

But she hadn't known the full cost of that imprisonment.

"The previous Celestial Emperor," Xiao Qing said, her voice hollow. "Where is he?"

"Dead," the woman said. "Or missing. Depends on who you ask. The current administration claims he ascended to a higher realm. Others say he was assassinated."

"And the agreement?"

"Void. The current celestial court doesn't recognize any agreements made by the previous administration. They've been systematically erasing the old records, rewriting history to suit their narrative."

Xiao Qing looked down at the scroll. At her sister's name. At the proof that everything she'd believed was both true and false.

Her sister was innocent.

Her sister had sacrificed herself.

And the celestial court had betrayed her.

"I need this scroll," Xiao Qing said.

"You can

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Weight of Memory

The forest swallowed her whole.

Xiao Qing moved through the ancient trees like a shadow, her feet barely touching the moss-covered ground. Three days since the prison. Three days since Elder Xu burned. Three days of running, of hiding, of feeling the celestial net tighten around her.

She'd stopped at a village the first night, hoping to blend in. But her reflection in a merchant's window had given her away—too still, too watchful, the kind of stillness that belonged to predators and immortals. The merchant had crossed himself and whispered a prayer to some local god. She'd left before he could raise an alarm.

Now she was deep in the wilds of the Southern Wastes, a place where even celestial guards hesitated to venture. The qi here was thick and strange, saturated with the death of old beasts and older gods. It pressed against her skin like wet cloth.

She found shelter in a cave half-hidden behind a waterfall. The spray cooled her face as she slipped inside, letting her eyes adjust to the darkness. The cave was small, barely tall enough to stand, but it would do.

She sat down, her back against the cold stone, and pulled out the jade pendant.

It had dimmed since the prison, its light reduced to a faint pulse, like a heartbeat slowing toward death. She'd used too much of its power to break those chains. But it was still alive. Still *her*.

"Where are you?" Xiao Qing whispered to the pendant. "Where did they take you?"

The pendant didn't answer. It never did. But sometimes, in her dreams, she thought she heard her sister's voice, distant and muffled, as if calling from the bottom of a deep well.

She closed her eyes.

---

The dream came quickly, as it always did.

*She was young again. No—she was younger. A green snake barely a century old, coiled in the branches of a peach tree, watching the world go by below. The world was simpler then. The sun was warmer. The mortals didn't fear her kind as much.*

*She heard footsteps and looked down.*

*Bai Suyin was walking through the garden, her white robes trailing behind her like clouds. She was beautiful in a way that hurt to look at—not because of her face, but because of the light that seemed to live inside her. Kindness, Xiao Qing would later learn to call it. Pure, unguarded kindness.*

*"Little Qing," Bai Suyin called up to her. "Come down. I've brought you something."*

*Xiao Qing slithered down the tree, shifting into her human form just before she hit the ground. She was clumsy at it then, always getting the proportions wrong. Today, her ears were too pointed, her hands too large.*

*Bai Suyin laughed, the sound like wind chimes. "You'll get it. Give it time."*

*"I don't want time," Xiao Qing pouted. "I want to be like you now."*

*"No you don't." Bai Suyin knelt down, her eyes softening. "You want to be yourself. The best version of yourself. That's different."*

*She pulled out a jade pendant, green and luminous, and placed it around Xiao Qing's neck. It was warm, as if it had been pressed against her sister's heart.*

*"This is for you," Bai Suyin said. "To remind you that you are never alone. No matter where I am, a part of me will always be with you."*

*Xiao Qing touched the pendant, feeling its power hum against her skin. "Where would you go?"*

*Bai Suyin's smile faltered, just for a moment. "I don't know. But the world is wide, Little Qing. And I have a feeling our paths will take us to strange places."*

*"Then I'll follow you," Xiao Qing said fiercely. "Wherever you go, I'll follow."*

*Bai Suyin pulled her into an embrace. "I know you will. That's what I'm afraid of."*

---

Xiao Qing woke with a gasp.

The pendant was hot against her chest, burning her skin. She clawed at it, but the chain wouldn't break, wouldn't let go. The heat spread through her body, searing her veins, her meridians, her dantian.

She screamed.

The cave walls shook. The waterfall outside seemed to pause, as if the world itself was holding its breath.

And then, as suddenly as it started, the heat stopped.

Xiao Qing collapsed, gasping, her body drenched in sweat. The pendant was cool now, almost cold. But something had changed.

She could feel it in her bones.

Her cultivation had been building for five centuries, slow and steady, like a river carving through stone. But now, the river had become a flood. Power surged through her, wild and untamed, threatening to tear her apart from the inside.

She looked at her hands. They were glowing.

Green light, bright and fierce, pulsed beneath her skin. She could see the veins, the bones, the meridians—all illuminated like rivers of jade.

"What have you done to me?" she whispered to the pendant.

The pendant didn't answer.

But something else did.

A voice, deep and ancient, echoed through the cave. It wasn't a sound she heard with her ears. It was a sound she felt in her soul.

*"You carry her blood. Her essence. Her burden."*

Xiao Qing spun around, her hand reaching for a weapon she didn't have. "Who's there?"

*"I am the memory of this place. The echo of what was."*

The shadows in the cave began to move, coalescing into a shape—vague, formless, but distinctly humanoid. It had no face, no features, but it radiated an age so vast it made Xiao Qing feel like an infant.

"What do you want?" she demanded.

*"To warn you."*

"About what?"

*"The pendant you carry is not merely a gift. It is a cage. Your sister did not give it to you out of love. She gave it to you to protect you from the truth."*

Xiao Qing's blood ran cold. "That's a lie."

*"Is it? Ask yourself this, child of the green snake: Why did Bai Suyin's cultivation grow so quickly? Why did she attract the attention of the celestial court? Why did Fahai pursue her with such obsession?"*

"Because she was powerful. Because she was kind. Because—"

*"Because she was not what she appeared to be."*

The shadow took a step closer. Xiao Qing backed away, her hand wrapping around the pendant.

*"Your sister was not a white snake, Xiao Qing. She was a fragment. A piece of something far older, far more dangerous. She was the key to a prison that holds something the celestial court fears above all else."*

"What are you talking about?"

*"The original sin. The first rebellion. The one that the gods erased from history."*

The shadow reached out, its hand passing through Xiao Qing's chest.

She felt it—a flood of images, sensations, memories that weren't her own.

*Fire. Blood. A woman with white hair and eyes like stars. A man with a serpent's tail and a dragon's roar. A battle that split the heavens. A seal that consumed a thousand lifetimes.*

*And in the center of it all, her sister. Bai Suyin. Not as Xiao Qing knew her, but as something else. Something terrible. Something beautiful.*

*Something that had been hiding.*

Xiao Qing fell to her knees, gasping.

*"The celestial court did not seal Bai Suyin because she loved a mortal,"* the shadow said. *"They sealed her because they discovered what she was. And what she was... is coming back."*

The shadow began to dissolve, its form breaking apart like mist in the wind.

*"Find the Library of Forgotten Things,"* it whispered. *"Find the truth. And prepare yourself, little snake. Because the war that your sister started is not over. It has only just begun."*

The shadow vanished.

Xiao Qing was alone.

She sat in the darkness for a long time, her mind reeling. The pendant was still warm, still comforting, but now it felt different. Heavy. Like a chain around her neck.

She thought about her sister's embrace, her soft voice, her gentle eyes. She thought about the way Bai Suyin had always seemed to know things she shouldn't, always been in the right place at the right time. She thought about the fear in her sister's eyes, that day in the garden, when she'd said, *"That's what I'm afraid of."*

Had Bai Suyin known? Had she been trying to protect Xiao Qing from this truth?

Or had she been using her?

Xiao Qing didn't know. And that uncertainty was worse than any enemy she'd ever faced.

She stood up, her legs shaking.

The Library of Forgotten Things. That's where the answers were. That's where the original registry was kept, the one that predated the current administration. The one that held the truth about her sister.

But to find it, she needed a key.

And the key required a sacrifice.

She stepped out of the cave, into the moonlight. The waterfall sprayed her face, cold and cleansing. She looked up at the sky, at the stars that had witnessed everything and said nothing.

"I don't know who you are anymore, sister," she whispered. "But I'm going to find out."

She walked into the forest, the pendant glowing against her chest.

Behind her, in the cave, the shadow reformed for just a moment.

It watched her go.

And it smiled.

---

In the celestial court, a bell tolled.

It was a sound that hadn't been heard in five hundred years, not since the day Bai Suyin was sealed. The gods stopped mid-sentence. The immortals turned pale. The guardians gripped their spears.

In the throne room, the Jade Emperor sat up straight, his ancient eyes narrowing.

"She's stirring," he said.

His advisor, a crane in human form, bowed low. "The seal is weakening, Your Majesty. The fragment we imprisoned is calling to its other pieces."

"How long?"

"Months. Perhaps weeks. The green snake has accelerated the timeline."

The Jade Emperor was silent for a long moment.

Then he stood.

"Summon Fahai."

The advisor's eyes widened. "But Your Majesty, Fahai has been in seclusion for—"

"Summon him."

The advisor bowed and withdrew.

The Jade Emperor turned to look out the window, at the mortal world far below, at the tiny speck of forest where a green snake was walking toward her destiny.

"Five hundred years," he murmured. "And still, you refuse to stay dead."

He raised his hand, and a golden bowl appeared in his palm. It was humming, vibrating with barely contained power.

"Come, Fahai," he said. "Your old enemy is waking. And this time, we will finish what we started."

The golden bowl glowed brighter, and in the depths of a mountain that no longer existed, a monk opened his eyes.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Keeper of Forgotten Things

The Library of Forgotten Things did not welcome visitors.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, then another two circling back east when the mountain paths turned impassable. She'd followed rumors whispered by spirits who owed her favors, by ghosts who remembered her sister's kindness, by a blind old turtle who'd once served in the celestial archives and knew the location of every hidden door in the three realms.

"The library finds you," the turtle had said, his voice like stones grinding together. "Not the other way around. You must be lost enough to be found. And you must carry something the library wants."

She'd asked what the library wanted.

The turtle had laughed, a wet, rattling sound. "Secrets. Always secrets."

Now she stood at the edge of a ravine that shouldn't exist. The map said there was a village here. The terrain said there was a river. But her feet stood on dry gravel, and before her stretched a crack in the earth so deep she couldn't see the bottom, and so narrow she'd have to turn sideways to enter.

The wind that rose from the ravine smelled of old paper and dust.

"Show yourself," Xiao Qing said.

Nothing.

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out Elder Xu's bone. It was warm against her palm, still humming with residual qi. She held it out over the abyss.

"I have a secret," she said. "The secret of how the celestial court rewrote its own history. I have the bone of a man who knew the truth. And I have five hundred years of rage that I'm willing to trade."

The ravine trembled.

A bridge of light formed beneath her feet—not solid, not quite there, but enough to walk on if she didn't look down. She stepped forward. The light held.

The descent took longer than it should have. Time moved strangely here, stretching and compressing like a snake coiling and uncoiling. She passed layers of reality: a forest where trees grew upside down, a desert made of mirrors, an ocean frozen mid-wave with fish suspended like jewels in ice.

And then she was there.

The Library of Forgotten Things wasn't a building. It was a space—a vast, impossible space where shelves stretched in every direction, including up and down. Books floated like birds. Scrolls hung from invisible threads. The air itself seemed made of words, half-formed sentences brushing against her skin like cobwebs.

In the center of it all sat a figure.

She'd expected a scholar, an ancient immortal, perhaps a dragon in human form. What she found was a child—a girl of perhaps twelve, with ink-stained fingers and eyes that held the weight of eternity. The girl sat cross-legged on a pile of books, reading by the light of a candle that burned without flame.

"You're early," the girl said without looking up.

Xiao Qing stopped. "I don't understand."

"Five hundred years, three months, and nine days." The girl turned a page. "That's when you were supposed to arrive. You're early by seventeen years. It's inconvenient."

"You knew I was coming?"

"I know everything that happens in the three realms." The girl finally looked up. Her eyes were black—not dark brown, not pupil-dilated, but entirely black, like voids where light simply stopped existing. "I just don't choose to act on most of it. You, however, are interesting."

Xiao Qing kept her hand near her sword. "Who are you?"

"I'm the Keeper. I don't have a name anymore. Names are for people who exist in linear time." The girl closed her book. "You want the original registry. The one that predates the celestial court's current administration."

"Yes."

"You understand the price."

"The key requires a sacrifice," Xiao Qing said. "I know."

The Keeper laughed. It was a child's laugh, high and sweet, and it made Xiao Qing's skin crawl. "Oh, you think you know. Everyone thinks they know. But the library doesn't want blood, little snake. It doesn't want years of your life or pieces of your soul. It wants truth."

"Truth?"

"Give me one thing you've never told anyone. One secret you've carried so long it's become part of your bones. Give me that, and I'll let you see the registry."

Xiao Qing's mouth went dry.

Five centuries of secrets. She had so many they felt like layers of skin, impossible to peel back without drawing blood. But there was one—one she'd never spoken aloud, not even to her sister.

"When I was young," she said slowly, "before I learned to cultivate, before I understood what I was—I killed my mother."

The Keeper's expression didn't change. "Go on."

"We were starving. The drought had lasted three years. My mother was already dying, wasting away from a sickness that had no cure. She asked me to end her suffering. I was seven years old. I didn't know any better." Xiao Qing's voice was flat, emotionless, the voice she used when discussing battle tactics. "I bit her throat and drank her blood. It was the first time I tasted power. And I liked it."

Silence.

The Keeper studied her for a long moment. Then she nodded once, sharply. "That's a good secret. The library accepts."

The ground beneath Xiao Qing's feet shifted. The shelves rearranged themselves, books flying past her head in a whirlwind of pages and leather. The Keeper raised her hand, and a single scroll descended from the darkness above—so old its edges were crumbling, so fragile it seemed held together by memory alone.

"The original registry," the Keeper said. "You have until the candle burns out."

Xiao Qing looked at the candle. It was half-melted, the wax pooling in uneven drips.

"How long?"

"However long it takes."

She grabbed the scroll and unrolled it.

The writing was ancient, predating the current celestial script by thousands of years. She had to squint to read it, picking out characters that had long since fallen out of use. But slowly, painfully, she began to understand.

The registry was a census of every being who had ever achieved immortality in the three realms. Names, dates, lineages, achievements. She scanned the entries, her heart pounding, until—

There.

Bai Suyin. White Snake. Fifth generation disciple of the Jade Pool lineage. Achieved immortality in the year of the Fire Rabbit, third moon, seventh day.

Xiao Qing read on.

Accomplishments: Mastery of water transformation. Healing arts second only to the Queen Mother herself. Mediation of the Dragon-Phoenix conflict of 2347.

Crimes: None recorded.

Sentence: Eternal imprisonment beneath Leifeng Pagoda.

Reason: —

The reason was blank. Not erased. Not redacted. Blank. As if someone had simply never filled it in.

Xiao Qing's hands shook. She read it again. The reason was blank.

"That's not possible," she whispered.

"Isn't it?" The Keeper's voice came from behind her. "Why do you think your sister never fought back? Why do you think she accepted her imprisonment without a single word of protest?"

Xiao Qing turned. The girl was standing now, her black eyes fixed on Xiao Qing with an intensity that made her want to look away.

"My sister was framed," Xiao Qing said. "Fahai—"

"Fahai was a tool. A weapon aimed in a direction he didn't understand." The Keeper stepped closer. "Your sister's crime wasn't written in the registry because it wasn't a crime. It was a choice."

"What choice?"

"She chose to protect you."

The words hit Xiao Qing like a physical blow. "What?"

"Five hundred years ago, the celestial court discovered that a demon was rising. Not an ordinary demon—something ancient, something from before the three realms were divided. A being of pure chaos that would destroy everything."

"I've never heard of such a thing."

"Of course you haven't. It was erased from history." The Keeper's voice dropped. "The court determined that the only way to stop it was to seal it away. But the seal required a willing sacrifice—someone with enough cultivation to contain the chaos, but enough humanity to be shaped by it."

Xiao Qing's mind raced. "You're saying my sister—"

"Your sister volunteered. She knew that if she went willingly, the chaos would be bound within her. But she also knew that the court would never admit what they'd done. They couldn't admit that they'd used a demon to save themselves. So they created a story. A love story. A tragic tale of a monk and a snake."

"But Fahai—"

"Fahai knew the truth. He was the one who performed the binding. And it destroyed him." The Keeper's expression softened, almost pityingly. "He's been trying to atone ever since."

Xiao Qing's legs gave out. She sank to her knees, the scroll falling from her hands.

All this time. All these centuries of rage, of plotting, of planning revenge against a man who was as much a victim as her sister.

"The chaos," she said hoarsely. "Is it still there? Inside my sister?"

"Yes."

"Can it be removed?"

The Keeper was quiet for so long that Xiao Qing looked up. The girl was staring at the candle. It was nearly burnt out.

"There is one way," the Keeper said. "But you won't like it."

"Tell me."

"The chaos can only be contained by a willing vessel. If you want to free your sister, you must take her place."

Xiao Qing's blood ran cold.

"Take her place? You mean—"

"Become the vessel. Let the chaos bind itself to you instead. Your sister would be freed, but you would be trapped beneath Leifeng Pagoda for eternity."

The candle flickered.

Outside the library, in the pocket of space between realms, something stirred. Something ancient. Something that had been waiting for Xiao Qing's arrival since before she was born.

The Keeper's eyes widened.

"You need to leave," she said. "Now."

"What?"

"The library isn't the only thing that can sense truth. You've spoken your secret. You've read the registry. And something else has noticed." The Keeper grabbed Xiao Qing's arm, her child's grip impossibly strong. "Something that wants to make sure you never free your sister."

The ground shook.

From the darkness above, a voice spoke—a voice that carried the weight of mountains and the cold of dead stars.

"Xiao Qing of the Green Snake lineage. You have been judged."

She looked up.

The celestial court had found her.

And at their head, standing in armor that blazed like the sun, was someone she recognized.

Someone she had hoped never to see again.

The General of the Southern Heavens.

Her sister's former lover.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Price of a Name

The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking south, following the pull of old blood and older magic. The jade pendant in her sleeve pulsed with each step, guiding her through forests that shifted when she blinked, across rivers that flowed uphill, past villages that appeared only in her peripheral vision and vanished when she turned to look.

She was being tested. The library only revealed itself to those who proved they deserved to find it.

On the third night, she stopped beneath a gnarled willow whose branches hung heavy with frost despite the summer heat. The pendant grew warm against her skin. She sat cross-legged at the tree's base and closed her eyes.

*You seek knowledge,* a voice said. It came from everywhere and nowhere. *But knowledge has a weight. Are you prepared to carry it?*

"I've been carrying it for five hundred years," Xiao Qing said. "Show me what you have."

The tree's roots shifted. A door opened in the earth, spiraling downward into darkness. She descended.

---

The Library of Forgotten Things was not a building. It was a wound in reality, a tear where memories went to die. Books floated in mid-air, their pages turning on their own. Scrolls hung from invisible threads. The air smelled of dust and lightning and something metallic that made her teeth ache.

A figure stood at the center of the chaos—an old woman with hair of silver and eyes of gold. She wore robes that shifted colors, never settling on one. When she smiled, her teeth were too sharp.

"Xiao Qing," the woman said. "I wondered when you'd come."

"Do I know you?"

"No. But I know you. I know everyone who comes here. I am the Librarian. I keep what the world forgets." She gestured to the floating books. "Everything that has been erased, rewritten, or buried—it all ends up here. Including your sister."

Xiao Qing's hand went to the jade pendant. "The original registry. Where is it?"

The Librarian's smile widened. "Straight to business. Good. I hate small talk." She turned and walked through the maze of floating texts. Xiao Qing followed.

They passed sections that made her skin crawl—books that whispered her name, scrolls that bled when she looked at them, a tome bound in what appeared to be human skin. The Librarian stopped before a pedestal made of black stone. On it sat a single scroll, yellowed with age, tied with a red ribbon.

"The original registry of the demon cultivation world," the Librarian said. "Dated seven hundred and twelve years ago. Before the celestial court's purge. Before your sister's name was stricken from every official record."

Xiao Qing reached for it. The Librarian's hand shot out and grabbed her wrist.

"Nothing in this library is free. You know that."

"What do you want?"

The Librarian studied her with those golden eyes. "Tell me a story I haven't heard."

"I don't have time for games."

"Neither do I. But I have eternity, and you don't." She released Xiao Qing's wrist. "Tell me a secret. One you've never told anyone. Something true. Something that hurts."

Xiao Qing stared at her. The silence stretched.

"I killed my mother," she said finally.

The Librarian's eyebrows rose. "Go on."

"She was a green snake of the Western Marshes. Weak. Cowardly. She abandoned my sister and me when we were still eggs. Left us to hatch alone in a pond that was drying up." Xiao Qing's voice was flat. "I found her three hundred years later, living in a human village, pretending to be mortal. She was married to a fisherman. Had two children. Human children."

"What did you do?"

"I waited until night. Until the children were asleep. Until the fisherman was drunk." Xiao Qing met the Librarian's eyes. "I asked her why she left us. She said she didn't remember any eggs. Said I must be mistaken. Said she'd never been a snake."

"And?"

"And I showed her what she'd forgotten." Xiao Qing's hand moved to her own throat, where a thin scar ran beneath her jaw. "She screamed. The fisherman woke up. I killed him first. Then I killed her. I burned the house down with their children inside."

The Librarian was quiet for a long moment. Then she laughed—a sharp, jagged sound. "Beautiful. The cruelty of abandoned children. I'll add it to my collection."

She stepped aside. The scroll was within reach.

Xiao Qing picked it up. The ribbon dissolved at her touch. She unrolled it, and the words glowed—names, hundreds of them, written in a script so old it predated the current celestial dynasty. She scanned down, her heart pounding.

*Lady Bai Suyin, Serpent of Mount Emei, Heir of the Western Water Dragon Lineage...*

Her sister's name. Still there. Still legible.

But beneath it, someone had written in fresh ink:

*NOTICE: This record has been superseded. Subject Bai Suyin is hereby classified as a Threat to Celestial Order. All prior achievements, titles, and honors are void. Punishment: Eternal imprisonment under Leifeng Pagoda. Terms: No visitation. No appeals. No exceptions.*

Xiao Qing's hands shook. "Who wrote this?"

"The current administration," the Librarian said. "They've been updating the old records for decades. Erasing anyone who opposed them. Your sister wasn't the first. She won't be the last."

"No visitation?"

"None permitted. The pagoda is warded against all forms of communication. Even the celestial court can't reach her."

"Then how do I free her?"

The Librarian tilted her head. "You don't. The pagoda was built by the Jade Emperor himself, reinforced by the Four Heavenly Kings, sealed with the blood of thirty-three celestial generals. It's not a prison. It's a tomb."

Xiao Qing rolled the scroll carefully. "There has to be a way."

"There is." The Librarian's voice dropped. "But you won't like it."

"Tell me."

"If you cannot reach your sister, you must bring the prison to you. Break the foundation of Leifeng Pagoda—not the structure itself, but the space it occupies. Collapse the boundary between her prison and the mortal realm."

"That would destroy everything within a thousand li."

"Yes."

Xiao Qing was silent.

"The celestial court would notice," the Librarian continued. "They would send every general, every god, every immortal under their command. You would face an army the likes of which hasn't been seen since the War of the Three Realms. And you would lose."

"Then why suggest it?"

"Because desperation makes us stupid." The Librarian smiled again, that sharp-toothed smile. "Or brave. Depends on the perspective."

Xiao Qing tucked the scroll into her sleeve. "Is there anything else I should know?"

The Librarian was quiet for a moment. "There is a boy."

"A boy?"

"Born fifteen years ago in a village on the eastern coast. He has no cultivation, no power, no name that matters. But he carries a fragment of your sister's soul."

Xiao Qing's blood went cold. "What?"

"The night she was sealed, she tore a piece of herself free. A sliver of her consciousness, wrapped in a thread of her qi. She sent it into the mortal realm, hoping it would find someone worthy. It found a newborn child instead."

"Where is he?"

"He's been in hiding his whole life. The celestial court doesn't know he exists. But they will, soon. Someone's been asking questions. Someone's been looking."

"Who?"

The Librarian's eyes flickered. "Someone who knows what he carries. Someone who wants to use him to reach your sister." She paused. "Someone who's been waiting for you to come out of hiding."

A chill ran down Xiao Qing's spine. "Show me."

The Librarian raised her hand. The floating books parted. A mirror appeared, its surface rippling like water. In it, Xiao Qing saw a village by the sea. A boy with dark hair and dark eyes, perhaps fourteen or fifteen, carrying a basket of fish. He looked up, as if sensing he was being watched.

His eyes were blue.

Bai Suyin's eyes.

"He doesn't know what he is," the Librarian said. "He thinks he's an orphan. His foster parents died three years ago in a plague. He's been surviving on his own."

"Who's looking for him?"

"A woman. Calls herself the Crimson Crane. Used to be a general in the celestial army, before she was exiled for treason. She's been building a rebellion in the shadows. She wants the boy—wants to use his connection to your sister to break the pagoda herself."

"For what purpose?"

"Power. Revenge. Who knows?" The Librarian shrugged. "She's been gathering artifacts, allies, secrets. She's patient. Smart. And she's been following your movements since you left Mount Emei."

Xiao Qing's hand went to her sword. "She's been watching me?"

"Of course. You're the only person who knows the boy exists. She needs you to lead her to him."

The mirror flickered. The image of the boy faded, replaced by a woman in crimson robes, standing on a cliff overlooking the sea. Her face was sharp, her eyes colder than winter. She was looking directly at the mirror.

At Xiao Qing.

"Found you," the woman mouthed.

The mirror shattered.

Xiao Qing stumbled back. Shards of glass fell at her feet, reflecting fragments of her face. The Librarian clapped her hands together, and the shards dissolved into mist.

"She knows you've seen her," the Librarian said. "She'll accelerate her plans now."

"How long do I have?"

"Three days. Maybe less. The boy is in a village called Tide's End, on the eastern coast. If the Crimson Crane reaches him before you do—"

"He'll be used as a weapon."

"Worse." The Librarian's voice was barely a whisper. "She'll kill him to extract the soul fragment. Your sister will die with him."

Xiao Qing turned toward the exit. The spiral staircase rose before her, leading back to the surface.

"One more thing," the Librarian called.

Xiao Qing paused.

"The boy's name. You'll need it to find him."

She waited.

The Librarian's smile was almost sad. "His name is Bai Yulong. White Dragon. Your sister gave him her name, her eyes, and a piece of her soul. She's been waiting for him to grow up."

Xiao Qing's heart clenched.

"Waiting for what?"

The Librarian's voice echoed as the library began to fade.

"For you to find him. For you to bring him home."

---

Xiao Qing emerged from the earth as the sun rose.

The forest was gone. She stood in a field of white flowers, their petals stained red at the edges. The pendant around her neck was glowing so bright it cast shadows.

She looked east, toward the sea.

Three days. A boy with her sister's eyes. A woman who wanted to use him.

Xiao Qing began to run.

Behind her, the Library of Forgotten Things folded itself back into nothing, leaving no trace that it had ever existed. The Librarian watched her go from the darkness between worlds, her golden eyes unblinking.

"Let's see if you're as dangerous as your sister," she murmured.

And in the shadows of the library, a scroll that Xiao Qing had missed unrolled itself. It was blank—except for a single line, written in blood:

*The Green Snake rises. The White Dragon waits

title: The Library's Price
content:
The mountain path twisted like a serpent's spine, narrow and treacherous in the growing darkness. Xiao Qing moved through the forest without sound, without shadow. Five centuries of practice had made her footsteps lighter than falling leaves, her breath quieter than wind through grass.

But her mind was not quiet.

Elder Xu's face swam before her eyes. The way he'd smiled when she'd freed him from the chains. The way he'd looked at her—not as a monster, not as a weapon, but as a student who had finally understood.

*"When fire is all you have, you burn everything."*

She'd thought he meant the celestial court. But standing in the ashes of his sacrifice, she wondered if he'd meant her.

The jade pendant pulsed warm against her chest, a steady heartbeat of stolen time. She'd used it to mask her presence, to slip past the celestial patrols that now swarmed the mountain like angry hornets. But its power was fading. She could feel it—the qi draining, the light dimming.

She needed the Library of Forgotten Things before the pendant failed completely.

The problem was finding it.

The Library existed in the space between spaces, a place that could only be reached through specific convergence points—locations where the veils between realms thinned to almost nothing. There were seven such points in the three realms. Three were guarded by celestial forces. Two had been destroyed in ancient wars. One was lost to time.

And one was in the belly of a mountain no map showed.

Xiao Qing stopped at the edge of a cliff, looking down into a valley shrouded in permanent mist. The locals called it the Valley of Echoing Silence—ironic, because nothing that entered ever made a sound again.

She'd been here once, three hundred years ago. She'd been chasing a rumor about her sister's original trial records, records that had supposedly been hidden in a monastery at the valley's heart. She'd found nothing but bones and the lingering scent of incense.

But she hadn't known what to look for then. She'd been desperate, grieving, half-mad with the need to find answers.

Now she was patient. Now she understood that some doors only opened when you stopped knocking.

She stepped off the cliff.

The fall lasted longer than it should have. The wind screamed past her ears, but the sound faded, stretched, became something else—a low hum that vibrated in her bones. The mist rose to meet her, cold and thick as water, and she plunged into it without resistance.

When she opened her eyes, she was standing in a courtyard.

The monastery was exactly as she remembered it—crumbling stones, broken statues, the skeletal remains of cherry trees that had died centuries ago. But there was something different now. A light in the main hall, where no light had been before.

Xiao Qing drew her sword. The blade whispered against the scabbard, a sound like silk tearing.

She walked toward the light.

The main hall's doors hung open, rotted wood creaking in a wind that didn't exist. Inside, a single candle burned on an altar that had been scrubbed clean. The walls were lined with scrolls—thousands of them, stacked in precise rows, their seals unbroken.

And at the center of the room, kneeling before the altar, was a woman.

She wore robes of pale gold, the color of autumn leaves catching sunlight. Her hair was white as snow, braided with threads of silver that caught the candlelight and scattered it like stars. She did not turn when Xiao Qing entered.

"I've been waiting," the woman said. Her voice was soft, melodic, the voice of someone who had not spoken aloud in a very long time. "I wondered if you would find your way here again, little snake."

Xiao Qing's grip tightened on her sword. "Who are you?"

The woman finally turned.

Her face was ageless—neither young nor old, beautiful in the way that mountains were beautiful, terrible in the way that storms were terrible. Her eyes were the color of deep water, fathomless and cold.

"I am the Keeper," she said. "And you have come to seek what was forgotten."

"You're the guardian of the Library?"

The Keeper smiled, a slight curve of lips that didn't reach her eyes. "I am the Library. Every scroll, every record, every truth that was deemed too dangerous to keep and too important to destroy—I am their memory given form. I have existed since before your sister was born, little snake. I will exist long after your bones have turned to dust."

Xiao Qing sheathed her sword. If this woman—this being—was truly the Library itself, violence would accomplish nothing. "I need the original registry. The one that predates the current celestial administration. The one that contains the truth about Bai Suyin's trial."

"I know what you need." The Keeper rose gracefully, her robes flowing around her like liquid gold. "The question is whether you are willing to pay the price."

"What price?"

"Every truth costs something. That is the first law of the Library. Some truths cost blood. Some cost memory. Some cost the very thing you're seeking to protect." The Keeper walked toward a wall of scrolls, her fingers trailing over the sealed ends. "The original registry is one of the oldest records in my collection. It predates the current celestial dynasty, predates the upheaval that placed the current Jade Emperor on his throne. It contains details that would shatter the foundations of the three realms."

"I know."

"And you still want it?"

"I need it."

The Keeper stopped, her hand hovering over a scroll bound in black silk. "The price, little snake, is this: you must give me one hundred years of your cultivation. Not your lifespan—your cultivation. The power you have spent five centuries gathering. You will lose all of it."

Xiao Qing's heart stopped. "All of it?"

"Every drop. Every breakthrough. Every moment of meditation, every battle fought, every lesson learned. You will return to the level of a newly awakened spirit beast. You will be weaker than you were when you first began your journey."

Five centuries. Five centuries of blood and sweat and tears, of pushing her body past its limits, of forcing her qi to flow through channels that had been closed since birth. Five centuries of becoming something more than a green snake, more than a sister, more than a footnote in a story that had never been about her.

And the Keeper was asking her to give it all up.

"There must be another way," Xiao Qing said.

"There is always another way. But the other ways will cost you more than cultivation." The Keeper's eyes were pitiless. "Your sister's trial was not a simple affair. It involved parties you cannot imagine, conspiracies that stretch back millennia. The truth is a weapon, little snake. And weapons must be paid for in blood or in time."

Xiao Qing thought of Elder Xu. Of the way he'd burned for her, of the single piece of bone she still carried in her sleeve. She thought of her sister, trapped under Leifeng Pagoda, waiting for a rescue that might never come.

"I'll pay it," she said.

The Keeper raised an eyebrow. "Without hesitation? Most petitioners bargain. They plead, they weep, they offer alternatives. You simply agree?"

"I've spent five centuries learning that hesitation costs more than action." Xiao Qing stepped forward. "Take the cultivation. Give me the registry."

"Very well."

The Keeper reached out and touched Xiao Qing's forehead with a single finger.

The pain was indescribable.

It felt like being skinned alive, like having her meridians ripped out one by one, like drowning in fire and freezing in ice simultaneously. Her cultivation—every drop of qi, every hard-won breakthrough, every moment of power—was torn from her body like roots from soil.

She screamed.

She couldn't help it. The sound tore from her throat, raw and animal, as her knees hit the stone floor. Her vision went white, then black, then white again. Her fingers clawed at the ground, leaving grooves in the ancient stone.

And then it was over.

Xiao Qing lay gasping on the floor, her body feeling wrong—too light, too empty, like a vessel that had been drained of all its contents. She could feel the absence of her qi like a phantom limb, a constant ache where power had once flowed.

She was weak. Pathetically, terrifyingly weak.

The Keeper held out a scroll bound in black silk. "The original registry. As promised."

Xiao Qing took it with shaking hands. The scroll was heavier than it looked, warm to the touch, pulsing with a faint energy that was not quite qi. She wanted to open it, to read the truth she had sacrificed everything for.

But she knew she couldn't. Not here. Not with the Keeper watching.

"Thank you," she said, and the words tasted like ash.

The Keeper tilted her head. "You will not thank me when you read what is inside. The truth is not kind, little snake. It does not care about your sacrifices or your pain. It simply is."

"I know."

"Do you?" The Keeper stepped closer, her eyes boring into Xiao Qing's. "Your sister was not the innocent you believe her to be. She made choices—terrible choices—that led to her imprisonment. The celestial court did not frame her. They simply recorded what she had done."

Xiao Qing's blood went cold. "You're lying."

"I cannot lie. I am the Library. I contain only truth." The Keeper's voice was gentle now, almost kind. "You have paid a great price for a truth that will break your heart. I hope you find it worth the cost."

The world twisted, warped, and Xiao Qing found herself standing at the edge of the cliff again, the scroll clutched to her chest, the mist of the valley swirling at her feet.

She was alone.

The pendant around her neck flickered, once, then went dark. Its power was spent.

And somewhere in the distance, she heard the sound of bells—celestial bells, ringing in alarm. They had found her trail. They were coming.

Xiao Qing looked down at the scroll in her hands.

She could read it now. She could learn the truth about her sister, the truth she had sacrificed five centuries of cultivation to obtain.

But the celestial guards were coming. And she was too weak to fight them.

She ran.

Behind her, in the Library of Forgotten Things, the Keeper watched through a mirror of still water. She watched the green snake flee into the darkness, clutching the scroll like a lifeline.

"You always did ask too many questions, little sister," the Keeper murmured.

And in the reflection, for just a moment, her eyes turned gold.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Price of Memory

The Library of Forgotten Things did not want to be found.

Xiao Qing had spent three days walking through the mountain passes of Kunlun, following threads of old qi that flickered like dying candle flames. Each time she thought she'd found the entrance, the path dissolved into mist, leaving her standing in empty fields or atop cliffs that dropped into nothing.

Her ribs ached constantly now. The healing had stopped progressing. Every time she pushed against the suppression wounds, they pushed back harder, reminding her that she was marked, hunted, running on borrowed time.

On the fourth night, she stopped trying to find the library and instead built a fire.

She sat cross-legged before the flames, the jade pendant warm against her chest, Elder Xu's bone fragment tucked into her sleeve. She didn't know why she'd kept it. Sentiment, perhaps. Or the desperate hope that some part of his cultivation might still be useful.

"You're doing it wrong."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. Xiao Qing's hand shot to her sword hilt before her mind registered that the speaker was not a threat.

A woman stepped out of the fire.

Not *from* the flames—but the flames themselves rose and shaped into a figure, orange and gold and white-hot. She was tall, with hair that moved like smoke and eyes that held the color of dying embers. She wore robes made of light, and where she walked, the grass did not burn but *remembered* being burned.

"I've been watching you for three days," the fire woman said. "You're getting colder. More desperate. The library can smell it on you, and it finds you… distasteful."

Xiao Qing did not lower her sword. "Who are you?"

"I have many names. The one you'd recognize is Zhaoyang. I was the first fire in the three realms. Before the sun. Before the celestial court. Before your sister was born, or her sister before her."

Xiao Qing's grip tightened. "You're a primordial."

"Don't use that word like it's a compliment." Zhaoyang circled the fire, her body shifting between solid and translucent. "I'm old. That's all it means. Old and tired and watching things I shouldn't care about. Like you."

"Why would a primordial care about a green snake?"

Zhaoyang laughed. It sounded like crackling wood. "Because you're interesting. Do you know how rare that is, after a million years? Everyone becomes predictable. The celestial court schemes in the same patterns. The demons rage in the same cycles. The mortals live and die in the same desperate little loops. But you—" She stopped, peering at Xiao Qing with those ember eyes. "You're doing something no one has done before."

"I'm trying to find my sister's true records."

"Anyone can want that. You're willing to burn for it. That's different." Zhaoyang gestured, and the fire rose higher. "The Library of Forgotten Things requires a sacrifice to enter. You know this. But you don't know *what* sacrifice it demands."

Xiao Qing sheathed her sword. If this primordial wanted her dead, she'd already be ash. "Tell me."

"The library doesn't take blood. It doesn't take years of life. It doesn't take treasure or power." Zhaoyang's smile was terrible and beautiful. "It takes *certainty*. Every time you enter, you must surrender one thing you believe absolutely to be true. And you must mean it. You can't lie to the library."

Xiao Qing felt her stomach drop. "What happens to the certainty?"

"It becomes part of the library. You forget you ever believed it. The belief becomes a book, shelved somewhere in the endless corridors, and you walk out with one less truth in your soul."

"That's dangerous."

"That's the point." Zhaoyang stepped closer, close enough that Xiao Qing felt the heat radiating from her. "What do you believe, green snake? What truth is so solid in your bones that you'd trade it for knowledge?"

*I believe my sister was framed.*

*I believe I can save her.*

*I believe I'm strong enough.*

*I believe I'm not alone.*

Xiao Qing didn't say any of these aloud. "I don't know what I'd give up."

"Then you're not ready." Zhaoyang began to fade, her form dissolving back into flame. "But I'll give you a hint. The library is nearby. I can feel it pressing against the world, waiting to be found. You have one day before it moves again. Find it, or don't. I don't care either way."

"Wait." Xiao Qing stepped forward. "Why are you helping me?"

Zhaoyang's eyes flickered, and for just a moment, the fire dimmed. "Because I knew your sister. Before the trial. Before Fahai. She was kind to me once, when kindness cost her everything. I owe her a debt." The flames shrank. "And because I hate what the celestial court has become. They've forgotten the old ways. They've forgotten that power without wisdom is just destruction waiting to happen."

Then she was gone, and the fire collapsed into ordinary embers.

Xiao Qing stood alone in the darkness, her heart pounding.

---

She found the library at dawn.

It wasn't a building. It was a *space*, a place where the world folded in on itself, where the morning light bent at impossible angles. The entrance was a doorway made of shadows, standing alone in a field of wildflowers. It hadn't been there a moment ago, and Xiao Qing knew that if she looked away, it would vanish.

She walked toward it, feeling the air grow thick and heavy. The doorway pulsed with a slow rhythm, like a heartbeat.

At the threshold, she stopped.

*What do I believe absolutely?*

She searched herself. Her convictions. Her truths.

*I believe the celestial court is corrupt.*

That was easy. Too easy. The library would take it, but it wouldn't cost her anything important.

*I believe Fahai is still alive.*

Possible. But she had no proof.

*I believe I will free my sister.*

That hurt to examine. Did she truly believe it? Or was it hope masquerading as certainty?

*I believe Xiao Qing is not my real name.*

The thought came unbidden, and she froze. It was true. She'd never had a name of her own. She'd been Xiao Qing—Little Qing—because she was the little sister, the green one, the one who followed. Even her cultivation name had been given by her sister's master, not chosen.

But that wasn't a certainty she wanted to trade.

She was still thinking when the doorway spoke.

"CHOOSE."

The voice was not a voice. It was a vibration in her bones, a pressure in her skull.

Xiao Qing reached into her sleeve and touched Elder Xu's bone fragment. She thought of his last words. *Remember who you are.*

She thought of her sister's face, serene and sad, as the chains wrapped around her.

She thought of five hundred years of loneliness, of training until her bones broke, of silence so deep she'd forgotten the sound of her own voice.

"I believe," she said slowly, "that I have been a ghost for five hundred years."

The doorway shuddered.

"Not a real ghost. But I've been living like one. Waiting. Hiding. Cultivating in isolation. I believed that if I stayed quiet and grew strong enough, I could fix everything. I believed that patience was virtue. I believed that my time would come if I just *waited* long enough."

She felt the words leaving her, felt the certainty peeling away like skin.

"That's what I give you. My belief that waiting was the right choice."

The doorway rippled, and for a moment, Xiao Qing felt herself *forgetting*. Something slipped away from her, a weight she hadn't known she was carrying. She blinked, and she was no longer sure what she'd been thinking about. Something about patience? About time? It didn't matter.

The doorway opened.

Inside, the Library of Forgotten Things stretched endlessly. Shelves rose into darkness, filled with books that glowed faintly. The air smelled of dust and age and secrets.

Xiao Qing stepped through.

She walked for what felt like hours, passing shelves labeled in languages she'd never seen. Some books were bound in leather, others in bark, others in what looked like solidified light. Every so often, she'd hear a whisper—a word, a name, a fragment of a memory—but when she turned, there was no one there.

The original registry would be in the oldest section. She knew this intuitively, the way a snake knows which direction leads to water. She followed the instinct deeper into the library, past shelves that grew older and older, until the books stopped having titles and started having *presences*.

She found the registry at the end of a corridor that existed outside of time.

It was not a book.

It was a *tree*.

A massive trunk rose from the floor, its bark inscribed with characters that shifted and writhed. Branches spread overhead, and from each branch hung fruits that pulsed with pale light. Each fruit was a record, a truth, a piece of celestial history.

Xiao Qing approached the tree, her hands shaking.

She found a fruit that glowed blue-white, the color of her sister's scales. It hung low, ripe, trembling as if it recognized her.

She reached out and touched it.

The fruit opened.

And her sister's voice filled her mind.

*"Xiao Qing. If you're hearing this, I'm already gone. But before they sealed me, I hid something. Something they'll never find. The truth about the celestial court's original sin. The reason they needed me gone. It's in the place where we first met, where the water meets the sky, where the lotus blooms in winter. Find it. But be careful—they'll be watching. They're always watching. And Xiao Qing? Don't trust the—”*

The voice cut off.

Xiao Qing stood frozen, the fruit's light fading, the message incomplete.

*Don't trust the what?*

She pulled her hand back, but the fruit had already closed. She tried again, but it wouldn't open. The message was sealed, one-time access, and she'd already used it.

She had a location. A clue. A warning cut short.

And she had no idea what she'd just given up to get it.

She turned to leave, her mind racing with possibilities. The place where water meets sky. The lotus that blooms in winter. She knew that place. It was where she and Bai Suyin had first met, centuries ago, in a lake that existed between realms.

She was halfway to the exit when she felt it.

*A presence.*

Not the library's. Something else. Something that had been waiting for her.

She turned.

A man stood at the end of the corridor. He was tall, dressed in robes of white and gold, his face obscured by a mask made of jade. He held a staff topped with a crystal that glowed with suppressed power.

"I wondered when you'd come," he said. His voice was smooth, ancient, terrible. "I've been waiting for this moment for a very long time, green snake."

Xiao Qing's hand went to her sword. "Who are you?"

The man stepped forward, and the library lights dimmed around him.

"I'm the one who erased your sister from the records in the first place."

He removed his mask.

And Xiao Qing felt the world tilt.

Because she knew that face. She'd seen it in portraits, in temples, in the oldest scrolls of the celestial court.

It was the face of the Celestial Emperor.

The one who was supposed to be dead.
